AI.method: Two-fluid analytic framework for circularly polarized Alfvén waves, supported by PIC simulations using the EPOCH code to study modulational instability and charge separation effects.
AI.result: Alfvén waves with wavenumber k ≤ k₀ experience powerful modulational instability; for highly magnetized plasma (σ≫1), high-frequency modes at k/k₀ ~ (2-3)×σ are quickly generated; for lower magnetization, the dominant mode satisfies Bragg condition k=2k₀; CP and LP waves show similar long-term behavior.
AI.conclusion: The modulational instability of Alfvén waves in pair plasmas is a robust phenomenon relevant to understanding FRB generation and propagation in magnetar environments.

title: Colloidal layer deposition with a controllable number of layers and compositional order
comment: These last two authors contributed equally to this work
summary: We design a system with a binary suspension of colloids and a surface that triggers the self-assembly of crystallites with a finite thickness. The proposed design allows controlling the number of layers forming the aggregate and constrains the two types of particles to lie on different planes. These functionalities are achieved by decorating the colloids and the surface with multiple DNA oligomers featuring specific interactions. The surface triggers a chain of reactions between DNA oligomers, leading to localized self-assembly. Equilibrium principles control the thickness of the aggregates. Instead, compositional order is achieved by engineering the reaction kinetics between DNA oligomers in a way that limits interactions between colloids of the same type. We validate our design using theory and reaction-diffusion simulation algorithms, which capture the multibody nature of the interactions. This work demonstrates how engineering the kinetics provides a new avenue for controlling the morphology of aggregates assembled by DNA.

AI.tldr: A system using DNA-decorated colloids and surfaces enables self-assembly of finite-thickness crystallites with compositional order, controlled by equilibrium thickness and kinetic-driven layer separation, validated through theory and simulations.
AI.motivation: To design a self-assembly system that controls both the thickness of crystallites and the spatial arrangement of different particle types within them.
AI.method: Binary colloidal suspension with DNA oligomers on particles and surface; equilibrium controls thickness while reaction kinetics between DNA oligomers controls compositional order; validated with theory and reaction-diffusion simulations.
AI.result: The design successfully achieves finite-thickness crystallites with controlled layering and type-specific plane segregation through combined equilibrium and kinetic engineering.
AI.conclusion: Engineering reaction kinetics provides a new avenue for controlling aggregate morphology in DNA-based self-assembly beyond equilibrium approaches.

title: Resolving an interface problem for the Dirac equation by using the unified transform method
summary: We use the Unified Transform Method (UTM) for the vector case to resolve an interface problem for the Dirac equation on two semi-infinite domains and two finite domains in the massless and massive cases, respectively. The UTM for the vector case is a variation of the UTM for the scalar case. The solutions obtained for an interface problem on two semi-infinite domains and two finite domains, respectively, in the massive case are convergent explicit integral representations.

AI.tldr: The paper applies the Unified Transform Method (UTM) for vector cases to solve interface problems for the Dirac equation on semi-infinite and finite domains, obtaining convergent explicit integral representations for massive cases.
AI.motivation: To extend the Unified Transform Method (UTM) from scalar to vector cases to resolve interface problems for the Dirac equation on combined domains.
AI.method: The authors use a vector variation of the UTM to solve interface problems for the Dirac equation on two semi-infinite domains and two finite domains, considering both massless and massive cases.
AI.result: Convergent explicit integral representations are obtained for the interface problems on semi-infinite and finite domains in the massive case.
AI.conclusion: The UTM for vector cases effectively resolves Dirac equation interface problems, producing convergent integral solutions for massive cases.

title: Discontinuity Analysis and Semi-Analytic Spectral Approximation for the Nonlocal Poisson Equation
summary: We study a nonlocal Poisson problem with discontinuous source term and analyze how the regularity of the integral kernel determines the discontinuity structure of the corresponding solution. Under general assumptions on compactly supported integrable kernels, we show that jump discontinuities in the source term are inherited by the solution. We then identify two principal mechanisms governing higher-order regularity: singular behavior of the kernel at the origin and jump discontinuities of the kernel, or of its derivatives, at the horizon endpoints. Singularities at the origin lead to blow-up of certain derivatives of the solution at the source discontinuity, while jumps at the horizon generate cascades of derivative discontinuities at translated locations. These phenomena occur for kernels commonly used in peridynamic-type models. By contrast, compactly supported \(C^\infty\) kernels do not generate derivative blow-up or cascading losses of regularity, and in this case the source term and the solution have equivalent piecewise smooth regularity. Motivated by this analysis, we develop a semi-analytic spectral method for the accurate numerical treatment of discontinuous nonlocal problems. The method uses successive smoothing transformations and explicitly constructed correction functions to convert the original problem into an auxiliary problem with improved regularity. A spectral solver is then applied to the smoothed problem, and the approximation to the original solution is recovered by adding back the analytic corrections. Numerical experiments show substantial gains in accuracy and convergence, demonstrating that the method effectively mitigates the loss of accuracy caused by discontinuities and Gibbs oscillations while retaining the efficiency of spectral methods.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes how kernel regularity determines solution discontinuity structure in nonlocal Poisson problems with discontinuous source terms, identifying mechanisms like singularities at origin and jumps at horizon that cause derivative blow-up or cascading discontinuities. The authors develop a semi-analytic spectral method using smoothing transformations and correction functions to accurately solve such problems, demonstrating significant improvements in accuracy and convergence.
AI.motivation: To understand how the regularity of integral kernels governs the discontinuity structure of solutions to nonlocal Poisson problems with discontinuous source terms, and to develop accurate numerical methods for such problems where discontinuities cause Gibbs oscillations and accuracy loss.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of kernel regularity effects on solution discontinuities, followed by a semi-analytic spectral method that uses successive smoothing transformations and explicitly constructed correction functions to convert the problem into an auxiliary problem with improved regularity, then applies a spectral solver and recovers the original solution by adding back analytic corrections.
AI.result: Jump discontinuities in source terms are inherited by solutions under general assumptions. Singularities at the origin cause derivative blow-up at source discontinuities; kernel jumps at horizon endpoints generate cascading derivative discontinuities at translated locations. Compactly supported C∞ kernels avoid these issues, preserving equivalent piecewise smooth regularity. The numerical method shows substantial gains in accuracy and convergence, effectively mitigating Gibbs oscillations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed semi-analytic spectral method effectively handles discontinuous nonlocal problems by leveraging theoretical understanding of kernel regularity effects, enabling accurate and efficient spectral computations that would otherwise suffer from discontinuity-induced accuracy loss.

title: MHD simulations on the large-scale propagation of high-speed solar wind streams
summary: We investigate the propagation of high-speed solar wind streams from their origin near the Sun to 1 AU using three-dimensional magnetohydrodynamic simulations. By tracking both global stream structure and individual plasma parcels, we assess how local in-situ measurements relate to the underlying plasma evolution. We find that high-speed streams are not parcel-preserving structures: commonly used diagnostics such as peak velocity, density, or temperature do not trace fixed plasma elements, and feature-based radial trends can therefore misrepresent the true evolution. Instead, velocity-based relationships provide a more robust framework for linking plasma parcels across heliocentric distances. Stream evolution is dominated by interaction regions, where compression leads to deceleration of fast wind, acceleration of slow wind, and significant heating. A boundary layer forms close to the Sun and can dominate narrow streams, biasing in-situ measurements toward lower apparent velocities. We show that three-dimensional transport, in particular latitudinal flows, redistributes mass and magnetic flux and reduces center-to-flank contrasts. While radial magnetic flux is conserved, the total field strength is not in spherical sampling geometries due to non-radial components. Finally, observed stream properties and geoeffectivity depend strongly on sampling location, stream geometry, and latitudinal magnetic deflection, introducing systematic variability and asymmetries in geomagnetic response.

AI.tldr: This study uses 3D MHD simulations to show that high-speed solar wind streams are not parcel-preserving; local in-situ measurements misrepresent true plasma evolution due to interaction regions, boundary layers, and 3D transport effects, with strong dependence on sampling geometry.
AI.motivation: To understand how local in-situ solar wind measurements relate to the actual plasma evolution, and to assess whether widely used diagnostics (peak velocity, density, temperature) accurately trace individual plasma parcels from the Sun to 1 AU.
AI.method: Three-dimensional magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) simulations tracking both global stream structure and individual plasma parcels from near the Sun to 1 AU.
AI.result: High-speed streams are not parcel-preserving; velocity-based relationships are more robust for linking plasma parcels; stream evolution is dominated by interaction regions (compression, deceleration, heating); boundary layers form near the Sun; 3D transport redistributes mass and magnetic flux; radial magnetic flux is conserved but total field strength is not; observed properties depend strongly on sampling location and geometry.
AI.conclusion: Feature-based radial trends can misrepresent true solar wind evolution; sampling geometry, stream structure, and latitudinal magnetic deflection introduce systematic variability and asymmetries in geomagnetic response.

title: Mesh Based Simulations with Spatial and Temporal awareness
summary: Machine Learning surrogates for Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), particularly Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and Transformers, have become a new important approach for accelerating physics simulations. However, we identify a critical bottleneck in the field: while architectures have advanced significantly, the common underlying training paradigms remain bound to naive assumptions, such as node-wise supervision and explicit Euler time-stepping. These legacy choices ignore the stiff dynamics and local flux continuity inherent to numerous partial differential equations resolution methods, such as Finite Element, Difference, or Volume (FEM). In this work, we propose a unified framework to bridge the gap between geometric deep learning and rigorous numerical analysis. We introduce three key innovations: (1) Multi Node Prediction, a stencil-level objective that predicts field values for a node's full local topology, enforcing spatial derivative consistency; (2) Temporal Correction, replacing unstable explicit schemes with a predictor-corrector via temporal Cross-Attention; and (3) Geometric Inductive Biases, leveraging 3D Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) to robustly capture rotational symmetries in unstructured meshes. We evaluate this framework across three architectures (MeshGraphNet, Transolver, and a Transformer) on diverse physics datasets. Our approach yields consistent improvements in accuracy and stability, particularly in long-horizon rollouts, while producing latent representations that generalize to unseen subtasks such as Wall Shear Stress or Pressure prediction. Code is available at https://github.com/DonsetPG/graph-physics.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a unified framework with three innovations (Multi Node Prediction, Temporal Correction, Geometric Inductive Biases) to improve GNN and Transformer surrogates for CFD simulations, achieving better accuracy, stability, and generalization across multiple architectures and datasets.
AI.motivation: Current ML surrogates for CFD (GNNs, Transformers) have advanced architecturally but still rely on naive training paradigms like node-wise supervision and explicit Euler time-stepping, which ignore stiff dynamics and local flux continuity inherent to PDE solvers like FEM.
AI.method: (1) Multi Node Prediction: stencil-level objective enforcing spatial derivative consistency; (2) Temporal Correction: predictor-corrector via temporal Cross-Attention replacing explicit schemes; (3) Geometric Inductive Biases: 3D Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) for rotational symmetries in unstructured meshes.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in accuracy and stability across three architectures (MeshGraphNet, Transolver, Transformer) on diverse physics datasets, especially in long-horizon rollouts, with latent representations generalizing to unseen tasks like Wall Shear Stress and Pressure prediction.
AI.conclusion: Bridging geometric deep learning with rigorous numerical analysis via stencil-level supervision, corrector-based time integration, and 3D RoPE significantly boosts surrogate performance and generalization for physics simulations.

title: Convexity inequalities for eigenvalues and log-concavity of eigenfunctions
summary: We give simple new proofs of two well-known results for the Schrödinger operator: first, the Brunn--Minkowski inequality for Dirichlet eigenvalues and, second, the log-concavity of the first Dirichlet eigenfunction. Our proof of the first applies to a class of domains including $C^{1,1}$ connected domains and convex potentials. In the special case of convex domains, the second result is a simple corollary.

AI.tldr: The paper presents simple new proofs for the Brunn-Minkowski inequality for Dirichlet eigenvalues and the log-concavity of the first Dirichlet eigenfunction for Schrödinger operators.
AI.motivation: To provide simpler, more accessible proofs of two well-known results in spectral theory for Schrödinger operators.
AI.method: The authors develop new proof techniques that apply to a broad class of domains (including C^{1,1} connected domains) with convex potentials, and show that for convex domains, the log-concavity result follows as a simple corollary.
AI.result: New simpler proofs are established for both the Brunn-Minkowski inequality for Dirichlet eigenvalues and the log-concavity of the first Dirichlet eigenfunction.
AI.conclusion: The work offers elegant alternative proofs that unify or simplify understanding of these key properties in spectral theory.

title: Error estimates for an unregularized optimal control problem for the stationary Navier-Stokes equations
summary: We consider an unregularized optimal control problem subject to the steady-state Navier-Stokes equations. We derive the existence of optimal solutions and prove first- and -- necessary and sufficient -- second-order optimality conditions. To approximate solutions to the optimal control problem, we consider the variational discretization scheme. We analyze convergence properties of the discretization and prove a priori error estimates for locally optimal controls that are nonsingular and which satisfy a growth condition which implies a bang-bang structure.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes optimal control problems governed by steady-state Navier-Stokes equations, proving existence of optimal solutions, optimality conditions, and convergence of variational discretization with error estimates for bang-bang controls.
AI.motivation: To develop and analyze optimal control theory for unregularized Navier-Stokes systems, particularly focusing on bang-bang structured controls.
AI.method: The authors derive first and second-order optimality conditions (both necessary and sufficient), apply variational discretization, and prove a priori error estimates for nonsingular locally optimal controls satisfying growth conditions.
AI.result: Existence of optimal solutions is established. Optimality conditions are rigorously proved. Convergence of the variational discretization is analyzed with error estimates for bang-bang type controls.
AI.conclusion: The variational discretization scheme converges for this class of problems, and error bounds can be derived for optimal controls with bang-bang structure under specified growth conditions.

title: Formation of Suprathermal Electron Populations in the Expanding, Turbulent Solar Wind
summary: Nonthermal features are ubiquitously observed in electron velocity distribution functions in the solar wind, yet their origin in the collisionless, turbulent, expanding solar-wind plasma remains unclear. We investigate how solar-wind expansion and Alfvénic turbulence jointly generate and regulate these features using the first fully kinetic particle-in-cell simulation of an expanding turbulent plasma under heliospheric conditions. In our setup, expansion-driven weakening of the magnetic field adiabatically cools the plasma perpendicularly to the mean field while leaving the parallel temperature largely unchanged, driving the system toward the firehose-instability threshold. Concurrently, strongly anisotropic turbulence leads to perpendicular heating and the development of nonthermal features. Subsequently, we find that suprathermal electron populations preferentially develop in the parallel direction, forming pronounced power-law tails even under weakly compressive, highly Alfvénic conditions, and persist despite anisotropy regulation by the firehose instability. The preferentially parallel energization suggests the involvement of parallel electric fields or resonant wave--particle interactions, rather than simple velocity-space redistribution. These results provide the first direct evidence of the emergence of nonthermal-electron features in a unified kinetic framework linking expansion, turbulence, and instabilities in the solar wind.

AI.tldr: This paper uses the first fully kinetic particle-in-cell simulation of an expanding turbulent plasma to show that solar-wind expansion and Alfvénic turbulence jointly generate nonthermal electron velocity distributions, with suprathermal electrons forming preferential parallel power-law tails that persist despite firehose instability regulation.
AI.motivation: Nonthermal features are commonly observed in solar-wind electron velocity distributions, but their origin in the collisionless, turbulent, expanding plasma remains unclear. The authors aim to understand how solar-wind expansion and Alfvénic turbulence jointly generate and regulate these features.
AI.method: The authors conduct the first fully kinetic particle-in-cell (PIC) simulation of an expanding turbulent plasma under heliospheric conditions, coupling solar-wind expansion with Alfvénic turbulence in a unified kinetic framework.
AI.result: Expansion adiabatically cools the plasma perpendicularly, driving the system toward the firehose-instability threshold. Turbulence causes perpendicular heating and nonthermal features. Suprathermal electrons preferentially develop in the parallel direction, forming power-law tails even under weakly compressive, highly Alfvénic conditions, persisting despite firehose-instability regulation.
AI.conclusion: The preferentially parallel energization suggests parallel electric fields or resonant wave-particle interactions are responsible, rather than simple velocity-space redistribution. This provides the first direct evidence of nonthermal-electron feature emergence in a unified kinetic framework linking expansion, turbulence, and instabilities in the solar wind.

title: Physics-Guided Deep Learning For High Resolution X-ray Imaging
summary: Imperfections in X-ray imaging systems can limit their performance, especially in High Energy Density (HED) or Inertial Fusion Energy (IFE)-relevant experiments that are typically single shot, by introducing structured, non-stationary features that overlap with the signal of interest. When the X-ray transmission is reconstructed by typical flat-field normalization, even small shot-to-shot drift of structured features imprints residual patterns onto transmission maps, degrading signal visibility and biasing measurements such as electron density, velocity and feature sizes. We investigate this limitation by modeling the artifacts as a separable feature layer and training a U-Net architecture to estimate and infer them directly from the experimental data. We compare our method against Fourier filtering and more advanced procedures like Dynamic Flat-Field Normalization (DFFN) to evaluate artifact suppression capability and signal preservation in the reconstructed transmission maps. In multiple synthetic injection tests, our Physics-Guided Deep Learning approach is able to obtain an improvement in mean Structural Similarity Index (SSIM) from 0.345 to 0.906 and from 0.0679 to 0.945, while better preserving filament profiles and reducing degradation of the filament signal during artifact suppression. Additionally, we utilize deep ensembles to obtain predictive epistemic uncertainty estimates for the U-Net based reconstruction, to ensure Out Of Distribution (OOD) robustness for this procedure.

AI.tldr: A Physics-Guided Deep Learning (U-Net) method removes structured, non-stationary artifacts from X-ray transmission images in HED/IFE experiments, outperforming traditional flat-field normalization and DFFN (improving SSIM from ~0.35 to ~0.91) while providing uncertainty estimates via deep ensembles.
AI.motivation: Imperfections in X-ray imaging systems introduce structured, non-stationary artifacts that degrade signal visibility and bias measurements (electron density, velocity, feature sizes) in single-shot HED/IFE experiments. Traditional flat-field normalization fails because shot-to-shot drift leaves residual patterns.
AI.method: Models artifacts as a separable feature layer, trains a U-Net architecture to estimate/infer artifacts directly from experimental data, and uses deep ensembles for epistemic uncertainty estimates to ensure OOD robustness. Compared against Fourier filtering and Dynamic Flat-Field Normalization (DFFN).
AI.result: Achieved SSIM improvement from 0.345→0.906 and 0.068→0.945 in synthetic injection tests. Better preserved filament profiles and reduced signal degradation during artifact suppression compared to Fourier filtering and DFFN.
AI.conclusion: Physics-Guided Deep Learning significantly outperforms existing methods for artifact suppression in X-ray transmission reconstruction for HED/IFE experiments, with deep ensembles providing reliable uncertainty quantification for OOD robustness.

title: On the stability of Lamb-Chaplygin dipole for the 2D Euler equation
summary: The Lamb-Chaplygin dipole is a traveling wave solution to the 2D incompressible Euler equation, whose orbital stability was established in [Abe-Choi, 2022] and [Abe-Choi-Jeong, 2025] assuming the odd symmetry in $x_2$ (O) and non-negativity in upper half-plane (N). This paper is devoted to further study of its stability in the following two aspects. Firstly, we prove the spectral stability of the linearized operator around the Lamb-Chaplygin dipole without conditions (O) or (N), based on the index theory established in [Lin-Zeng, 2022]. This excludes an instability mechanism by unstable eigenmodes, and provides rigorous evidence towards nonlinear stability in this general setting. Secondly, assuming (O) and (N), we refine the orbital stability results in [Abe-Choi, 2022] and [Abe-Choi-Jeong, 2025] quantitatively by proving a linear bound of the fluctuation and a uniform control of the moving velocity. Instead of using a variational approach, our proof relies on the construction of a new coercive Lyapunov functional with a delicate mixed structure: it is quadratic in the interior region, but linear in the exterior region.

AI.tldr: This paper studies stability of the Lamb-Chaplygin dipole (a 2D Euler traveling wave solution), proving spectral stability without symmetry/positivity conditions and refining orbital stability bounds with a new mixed-structure Lyapunov functional.
AI.motivation: To advance understanding of the Lamb-Chaplygin dipole's stability beyond prior results that relied on odd symmetry (O) and non-negativity (N) conditions, by proving spectral stability in a general setting and providing quantitative refinements to orbital stability.
AI.method: Spectral stability is proved via index theory from Lin-Zeng (2022). Orbital stability refinement uses a novel coercive Lyapunov functional with a mixed quadratic (interior) / linear (exterior) structure, replacing the variational approach.
AI.result: (1) Spectral stability of the linearized operator without (O)/(N) conditions, ruling out unstable eigenmodes. (2) Under (O)/(N), a linear bound on fluctuations and uniform control of moving velocity, quantitatively improving prior orbital stability results.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides rigorous evidence toward nonlinear stability in a general setting and achieves sharper quantitative stability estimates under symmetry/positivity assumptions through a novel Lyapunov functional design.

title: Maxwell à la Helmholtz: Direct boundary integral equations for 3D scattering by perfect electric conductors via Helmholtz operators
summary: This paper is the direct-formulation companion to [Burbano-Gallegos, P'erez-Arancibia, and Turc, ESAIM: M2AN, 60(1):273--315, 2026], which developed indirect combined-field-only boundary integral equations (BIEs) for time-harmonic electromagnetic scattering by smooth perfectly electrically conducting (PEC) obstacles, relying entirely on Helmholtz boundary integral operators. Here we exploit the same equivalence between the Maxwell PEC scattering problem and a pair of vector Helmholtz boundary value problems -- one for the electric field and one for the magnetic field -- to derive direct BIE formulations whose unknowns are the Dirichlet and Neumann traces of the total fields, decomposed into their normal and tangential surface components. These unknowns carry direct physical meaning: in particular, the magnetic-field formulation yields the surface electric currents as part of its solution. The mixed regularity of the two field-trace components requires introducing a tailored product H"older space, a distinctive feature absent from the indirect approach. We prove that the resulting Direct Electric and Magnetic Combined-Field-Only Integral Equations (D-ECFOIE and D-MCFOIE) are uniquely solvable at all frequencies, and introduce Calder'on-type regularizations (RD-ECFOIE and RD-MCFOIE) that render them of the Fredholm second kind. We further examine the low-frequency breakdown affecting the electric-field formulation and introduce a modified equation that enforces the physical charge-conservation constraints, which restores numerical accuracy and well-conditioned linear systems for frequencies arbitrarily close to zero. Numerical experiments, performed using a high-order Nystr"om solver based on the Density Interpolation Method and implemented in the Julia package Inti.jl, validate the accuracy and robustness of the proposed formulations across a range of geometries and frequencies.

AI.tldr: This paper presents direct combined-field-only boundary integral equation formulations (D-ECFOIE and D-MCFOIE) for time-harmonic electromagnetic scattering by PEC obstacles, using Helmholtz BIE operators. It introduces a tailored product Hölder space for mixed-regularity unknowns, proves unique solvability at all frequencies, adds Calderón-type regularizations to achieve Fredholm second-kind form, and fixes low-frequency breakdown in the electric formulation via charge-conservation constraints. Numerical tests with a high-order Nyström solver validate the approach.
AI.motivation: To develop direct BIE formulations for Maxwell PEC scattering that use only Helmholtz boundary integral operators (avoiding Maxwell-specific operators), yield unknowns with direct physical meaning (e.g., surface electric currents), and ensure unique solvability at all frequencies including near-zero frequencies.
AI.method: Leverages the equivalence between Maxwell PEC scattering and two vector Helmholtz BVPs (electric and magnetic fields). Introduces a product Hölder space to handle mixed regularity of normal and tangential trace components. Proves unique solvability, applies Calderón-type regularizations for Fredholm second-kind equations, and modifies the electric formulation with charge-conservation constraints to address low-frequency breakdown. Numerically implemented via high-order Nyström method with Density Interpolation Method in Julia package Inti.jl.
AI.result: Unique solvability is proven at all frequencies for both D-ECFOIE and D-MCFOIE. Calderón regularizations yield Fredholm second-kind equations. The low-frequency breakdown in the electric formulation is resolved by enforcing charge conservation. Numerical experiments validate accuracy and robustness across various geometries and frequencies.
AI.conclusion: The proposed direct BIE formulations are viable, accurate, and robust alternatives for solving time-harmonic Maxwell PEC scattering problems, with advantages over the indirect approach in providing physically meaningful unknowns and maintaining good conditioning even at near-zero frequencies.

title: Revisiting the Rheology of Neutron Star Crusts with Molecular Dynamics
comment: 3 pages, 1 figure, accepted for publication in RNAAS
summary: Explosive events from magnetars are likely due to the catastrophic release of stress in their crusts, but the behavior of crustal matter beyond linear elasticity is poorly understood. We argue here that seminal results from molecular dynamics informing crust breaking calculations are non-converged, and must be revisited. We estimate the criteria for quasi-static, rate-independent flow by comparing imposed deformation timescales to grain boundary diffusion in polycrystals. We argue that convergence in this regime should be observed at strain rates slower than $10^{-5}\,ω_p$ (plasma frequency $ω_p$) in simulations of $N\approx10^5$ particles across order 10 grains at a quarter of the melting temperature. Though computationally expensive, this is tractable with modern methods and GPU supercomputers.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that previous molecular dynamics simulations of magnetar crust breaking are non-converged and need revisiting, and proposes criteria for achieving convergence in quasi-static flow simulations.
AI.motivation: Explosive events from magnetars are likely due to catastrophic stress release in their crusts, but the behavior of crustal matter beyond linear elasticity is poorly understood.
AI.method: The authors estimate convergence criteria for quasi-static, rate-independent flow by comparing imposed deformation timescales to grain boundary diffusion in polycrystals. They propose specific simulation parameters: strain rates slower than 10⁻⁵ ω_p, N≈10⁵ particles across ~10 grains at a quarter of the melting temperature.
AI.result: Previous molecular dynamics results for crust breaking calculations are non-converged. Proposed criteria require computationally expensive but tractable simulations using modern methods and GPU supercomputers.
AI.conclusion: Converged simulations of magnetar crust breaking are possible with modern GPU supercomputers, though computationally demanding.

title: Optimal transport of signed measures: existence, uniqueness and fractal structure
comment: Keywords: Optimal transport, signed measures, Monge-Ampere equation, fractal sets, Ahlfors-regular sets, adaptive regularization
summary: This paper develops a comprehensive theory of optimal transport for signed (real) measures on Rd. Extending the classical Brenier theorem, we consider Jordan decompositions of measures with possibly fractal singular parts. Under suitable regularity and structural assumptions (H1-H5), we prove existence and uniqueness of an optimal transport map T for a cost that distinguishes same-sign and opposite sign transports via a positional penalty lambda. We derive coupled Monge-Ampere equations and a double Legendre transform system characterizing the solution. Moreover, we show that the optimal transport preserves Hausdorff dimension and Ahlfors regularity of fractal sets. The proof relies on a novel adaptive regularization technique that respects the signed and fractal nature of the measures.

AI.tldr: This paper extends optimal transport theory to signed measures with fractal singular parts, proving existence and uniqueness of an optimal transport map under specific assumptions, deriving coupled Monge-Ampere equations, and showing preservation of fractal dimensions.
AI.motivation: To extend classical optimal transport theory (Brenier theorem) from positive measures to signed (real) measures with possible fractal singular components, addressing a gap in existing theory.
AI.method: The authors introduce a cost function with positional penalty lambda distinguishing same-sign vs. opposite-sign transport, develop coupled Monge-Ampere equations and a double Legendre transform system, and employ a novel adaptive regularization technique that respects signed and fractal measure structures.
AI.result: Existence and uniqueness of an optimal transport map for signed measures under conditions H1-H5; optimal transport preserves Hausdorff dimension and Ahlfors regularity of fractal sets.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a robust theoretical foundation for optimal transport on signed measures with fractal geometry, with potential applications in imaging, PDEs, and geometric measure theory.

title: Error analysis of a divergence-preserving mixed finite element scheme for the incompressible Hall--magnetohydrodynamic equations
summary: The incompressible Hall-magnetohydrodynamics (Hall--MHD) system presents substantial analytical and computational challenges due to its stiff, highly nonlinear Hall term and the strict requirement that the magnetic field remains solenoidal. In this paper, we study a Voigt-regularised Hall--MHD system, which is of independent analytical interest and provides a physically consistent, well-posed regularisation of the original model. We propose, analyse, and implement a structure-preserving, linear, fully discrete finite element method for this regularised problem. Using finite element exterior calculus and a mixed formulation, the spatial discretisation enforces the divergence-free condition on the magnetic field exactly, while a skew-symmetric, linearly implicit time discretisation yields unconditional energy stability. We establish optimal convergence rates for the Voigt-regularised problem and, additionally, derive error estimates for the unregularised Hall--MHD system, with the Voigt regularisation playing a crucial role in the non-resistive regime. Finally, numerical simulations in both 2.5D and 3D corroborate the theoretical results and demonstrate the physical fidelity of the scheme.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes, analyzes, and implements a structure-preserving, linear, fully discrete finite element method for a Voigt-regularised Hall-MHD system, achieving optimal convergence rates and exact divergence-free magnetic fields.
AI.motivation: The Hall-MHD system is analytically and computationally challenging due to its stiff, nonlinear Hall term and the strict solenoidality requirement for the magnetic field. A regularisation that maintains physical consistency is needed.
AI.method: The authors use Voigt regularisation combined with finite element exterior calculus and a mixed formulation for spatial discretisation, ensuring exact divergence-free magnetic fields. A skew-symmetric, linearly implicit time discretisation achieves unconditional energy stability.
AI.result: Optimal convergence rates are established for the Voigt-regularised problem, and error estimates are derived for the unregularised Hall-MHD system. Numerical simulations in 2.5D and 3D confirm theoretical results and physical fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method is physically consistent, well-posed, and structure-preserving, with the Voigt regularisation playing a crucial role especially in the non-resistive regime.

title: State-Specific Kinetic Modeling of Atomic H for H$_2$/ He Entry Flows
summary: An 11-species thermochemical model for H$_2$/ He mixtures with state-specific kinetics for atomic H is developed and used to simulate 1-D shocks at conditions relevant for ice and gas giant entry flows. To implement this kinetic model, a literature review of the state-specific excitation and ionization rate constants of atomic H is first performed. While electron-impact rate constants from various sources are found to be in good agreement, large discrepancies are found in the limited data available on heavy-particle-impact rate constants. To validate the kinetic model, 1-D steady shocks are simulated using a space-marching code that explicitly accounts for shock tube boundary layer effects. The resulting radiance profiles are compared to experimental data from the NASA Ames Electric Arc Shock Tube (EAST) facility, and are found to reproduce the measured values reasonably accurately while capturing the distinct induction zone behavior observed in the experiments. A sensitivity analysis of the kinetic rates and boundary layer treatment reveals avenues for further improvement of the model. Finally, a comparison to alternate models from the literature underscores the improved accuracy of the present model in predicting ionization and radiation profiles.

AI.tldr: An 11-species thermochemical model for H₂/He mixtures with state-specific kinetics for atomic H is developed, validated against NASA Ames EAST shock tube data, and shows improved accuracy in predicting ionization and radiation profiles for ice and gas giant entry flows.
AI.motivation: To improve predictive capabilities for ice and gas giant entry flows by developing a more accurate kinetic model for H₂/He mixtures, addressing large discrepancies found in heavy-particle-impact rate constants from literature.
AI.method: Developed an 11-species thermochemical model with state-specific kinetics for atomic H; conducted literature review of rate constants; simulated 1-D steady shocks using space-marching code with shock tube boundary layer effects; validated against EAST experimental radiance data.
AI.result: The model reproduces EAST measured radiance values reasonably accurately, captures distinct induction zone behavior observed in experiments, and shows improved accuracy over alternate models in predicting ionization and radiation profiles.
AI.conclusion: The model offers improved accuracy for ice and gas giant entry flow predictions, with a sensitivity analysis identifying avenues for further improvement, particularly regarding heavy-particle-impact rate constants and boundary layer treatment.

title: A Fully Ab-Initio Spin-Lattice Dynamics Framework for Magnetic Materials
summary: Coupled spin-lattice dynamics (SLD) underlie a wide range of magnetic phenomena, yet a unified first-principles framework that propagates both degrees of freedom without empirical parameterization has remained elusive. We present a fully ab initio SLD approach integrated into VASP, in which interatomic forces and effective magnetic fields are obtained at each time step from self-consistent constrained-moment density-functional calculations. The method is validated on four materials spanning ferromagnetic, non-collinear, and geometrically frustrated orders, recovering the correct magnetic ground state in every case from random initial conditions. SLD trajectories also provide physically correlated training data for magnetic machine-learning potentials, as demonstrated for BiFeO$_3$ by a reduction of up to approximately one order of magnitude in energy MAE over training on randomized spin configurations. This framework opens a practical first-principles route to finite-temperature spin-lattice coupled phenomena in magnetic materials.

AI.tldr: A fully ab initio coupled spin-lattice dynamics (SLD) approach is implemented in VASP, using self-constrained density-functional calculations to propagate spins and lattice without empirical parameters. Validated on four magnetic materials, it recovers correct ground states and enables better ML potentials for spin-lattice systems.
AI.motivation: Coupled spin-lattice dynamics are important for magnetic phenomena, but no unified first-principles framework existed that propagates both degrees of freedom without empirical parameterization, motivating the development of a fully ab initio approach.
AI.method: The authors integrated a fully ab initio SLD approach into VASP, where interatomic forces and effective magnetic fields are computed at each time step from self-consistent constrained-moment density-functional theory (DFT) calculations, avoiding any empirical parameters.
AI.result: Validated on four materials (ferromagnetic, non-collinear, geometrically frustrated), the method recovers correct magnetic ground states from random initial conditions. SLD trajectories also reduced energy MAE by ~1 order of magnitude for ML potentials of BiFeO₃ compared to random spin configurations.
AI.conclusion: This framework provides a practical first-principles route to study finite-temperature spin-lattice coupled phenomena in magnetic materials, and generates physically correlated training data for magnetic machine-learning potentials.

title: Rigidity for the Pólya-Szegö inequality under circular rearrangement
summary: A Pólya-Szegö inequality for the circular rearrangement is proven, under general assumptions. In addition, sufficient conditions are given, under which all the extremals of the inequality are symmetric.

AI.tldr: A Pólya-Szegö inequality for circular rearrangement is proven under general assumptions, with sufficient conditions provided for symmetry of all extremals.
AI.motivation: To generalize the Pólya-Szegö inequality to circular rearrangements and identify conditions under which extremals are symmetric.
AI.method: The paper proves a Pólya-Szegö inequality for the circular rearrangement under general assumptions, and establishes sufficient conditions for the symmetry of all extremals.
AI.result: A Pólya-Szegö inequality is proven for circular rearrangement; sufficient conditions for symmetric extremals are identified.
AI.conclusion: The inequality holds generally, and symmetry of extremals is guaranteed under the given sufficient conditions.

title: Numerical Construction of Elliptic Lower-Dimensional Quasi-Periodic Solutions with a Priori Bound
summary: A numerical framework for constructing full-dimensional quasi-periodic solutions in nearly integrable systems was recently developed by Fu and Shi[2026]. Based on an alternating scheme, this approach effectively overcomes the secular drift in angle variables, a fundamental limitation of symplectic integrators. However, in many applications, such as the restricted three-body problem, lower-dimensional quasi-periodic solutions hold greater significance. The construction of these solutions is considerably more challenging due to the presence of normal frequencies, leading to intricate resonance phenomena. Beyond the subspace resonance, one must also account for the first and second Melnikov conditions to eliminate small divisors. In this study, we extend the proposed alternating numerical scheme to compute the elliptic lower-dimensional quasi-periodic solutions. Numerical experiments are presented for the Hénon-Heiles model and the Fermi--Pasta--Ulam (FPU) model, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method. Furthermore, we emphasize that the perturbation is not merely a polynomial with real coefficients but is a real-valued function. As a result, the associated perturbation operator exhibits Gevrey decay without possessing a Hankel structure. Meanwhile, we further simplify the multi-scale analysis by exploiting the resolvent identity, showing that the global inverse can be expressed linearly in terms of local inverses via the gluing procedure. This representation reveals a regime-dependent interaction structure: weak interactions dominate at short range, while strong interactions emerge at long range. This balance ensures that the Gevrey decay of the inverse remains uniformly controlled. Moreover, within this linear representation, the inversion conditions provide a clearer characterization of the localization properties.

AI.tldr: This paper extends an alternating numerical scheme to compute lower-dimensional quasi-periodic solutions in nearly integrable systems, addressing challenges like normal frequencies and resonance conditions. It validates the method on Hénon-Heiles and FPU models, and uses resolvent identity to analyze Gevrey decay and inversion properties.
AI.motivation: Lower-dimensional quasi-periodic solutions are more significant in applications like the restricted three-body problem but are harder to construct due to normal frequencies, resonance phenomena, and Melnikov conditions. Prior work by Fu and Shi[2026] on full-dimensional solutions served as the starting point.
AI.method: Extends the alternating numerical scheme of Fu and Shi to elliptic lower-dimensional quasi-periodic solutions. Incorporates subspace resonance and first/second Melnikov conditions. Uses resolvent identity to simplify multi-scale analysis, expressing global inverse linearly in terms of local inverses via gluing.
AI.result: Validated on Hénon-Heiles and FPU models. The linear representation via resolvent identity reveals a regime-dependent interaction structure (weak short-range, strong long-range), ensuring uniformly controlled Gevrey decay of the inverse.
AI.conclusion: The extended method effectively computes lower-dimensional quasi-periodic solutions, and the resolvent-based analysis provides clearer characterization of localization properties and inversion conditions.

title: Polymorphic crystallites model for monolayer amorphous materials
comment: 22 pages, 4 figures and 1 table(main text) + 7 pages Supporting Information
summary: Modeling the atomic structure of amorphous materials has long been a critical challenge in materials science. Recent advances in monolayer amorphous materials enable direct observation of their atomic structures, paving the way for a better understanding of their atomic-scale models. Here, we investigate amorphous multielement monolayers using machine learning potential from first-principles total energies via energy-driven kinetic Monte Carlo based active-learning framework. A polymorphic crystallite model is proposed to describe the atomic configuration of monolayer amorphous boron nitride, as it consists of coexisting crystallite of $o-B_2N_2$ and $o-B_4N_4$ structural motifs. Generality of the polymorphic crystallite model is further validated in two other multielement monolayer amorphous systems. Monolayer amorphous LiCl shows coexisting hexagonal and tetragonal crystallites, while monolayer amorphous BCN contains a combination of graphene-like, h-BN-like, and borophene-like crystallites. These findings expand the classical picture of amorphous structure models and offer new insight into the microscopic structure of amorphous materials.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a polymorphic crystallite model for amorphous monolayer materials (BN, LiCl, BCN) using machine learning potentials and active-learning frameworks, revealing coexisting crystalline structural motifs that expand classical amorphous structure models.
AI.motivation: Modeling atomic structures of amorphous materials has been a long-standing challenge; recent experimental advances in monolayer amorphous materials enable direct observation, motivating the need for better atomic-scale models.
AI.method: The authors use machine learning potential derived from first-principles total energies via an energy-driven kinetic Monte Carlo based active-learning framework to model amorphous multielement monolayers.
AI.result: A polymorphic crystallite model is proposed: monolayer amorphous BN consists of coexisting o-B₂N₂ and o-B₄N₄ crystallites; monolayer amorphous LiCl shows coexisting hexagonal and tetragonal crystallites; monolayer amorphous BCN contains graphene-like, h-BN-like, and borophene-like crystallites.
AI.conclusion: The polymorphic crystallite model expands classical amorphous structure models and offers new insights into the microscopic structure of amorphous materials, with generality validated across multiple multielement monolayer systems.

title: Relaxation dynamics of the Inertial Winfree model
summary: We prove two synchronization theorems for the second-order (inertial) Winfree model of coupled oscillators. The first result is a pathwise oscillator-death theorem with explicit smallness thresholds on the natural frequencies, initial velocities, and inertia, scaling as $R_0^{3/2}$ in the initial order parameter $R_0$. The second result is a qualitative zero-inertia synchronization statement: under generic initial data, if the intrinsic and initial velocity spreads are small compared to $κ$ and the inertia $m$ is small, then the limiting order parameter can be made arbitrarily close to 2. The proof of the first result is organized around three mechanisms, namely inertial gradient flow and the Łojasiewicz theorem, an initial layer argument, and an order-parameter bootstrapping argument. The proof of the second result involves approximation to the first-order case via a quantitative Tikhonov theorem.

AI.tldr: This paper proves two synchronization theorems for the second-order inertial Winfree model. The first establishes a pathwise oscillator-death theorem with explicit smallness thresholds scaling as $R_0^{3/2}$, and the second shows that with small inertia and velocity spreads, the limiting order parameter approaches 2.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous synchronization conditions for the second-order (inertial) Winfree model of coupled oscillators, extending existing results from the first-order model.
AI.method: For Theorem 1: inertial gradient flow with Łojasiewicz theorem, initial layer argument, and order-parameter bootstrapping. For Theorem 2: approximation to first-order case via a quantitative Tikhonov theorem.
AI.result: Theorem 1: pathwise oscillator-death with thresholds scaling as $R_0^{3/2}$. Theorem 2: under small inertia and velocity spreads, limiting order parameter approaches 2.
AI.conclusion: The second-order Winfree model admits synchronization under certain smallness conditions, with the first result being quantitative and the second being qualitative but showing arbitrarily close synchronization to the maximal order parameter.

title: On the Practical Impact of Local Linear Instabilities in Entropy-Stable Schemes
summary: Local linear instability refers to the linearized discrete operator exhibiting perturbation growth exceeding that of the corresponding continuous linearized problem. In the context of nonlinear entropy-stable discretizations, we argue that local linear instabilities should be interpreted as a source of numerical error whose practical impact is often negligible compared with other discretization errors. For split-form discretizations of the variable-coefficient linear advection equation, such as those resulting from linearizations of entropy-stable discretizations of the Burgers equation, perturbations can indeed exhibit unphysical modal growth. However, we demonstrate that this growth satisfies physically interpretable bounds and is typically small. Furthermore, through modified-equation analysis and numerical experiments, we show that the growth is dominated by highly oscillatory and boundary-localized unphysical modes, and can therefore be readily controlled by small amounts of numerical dissipation. More generally, this modal perturbation growth does not extend directly to nonlinear two-point-flux discretizations of the type used in entropy-stable discretizations of the Euler equations. Floquet analysis demonstrates that unstable spectra of frozen-baseflow Jacobians need not lead to unstable perturbation growth. Using the geometric flux for the variable-coefficient linear advection equation, we derive a sharp perturbation growth bound predicting negligible growth, then show analogous behaviour for the logarithmic flux numerically. Finally, we argue that robustness issues observed for entropy-stable schemes in density-wave problems are better attributed to poor near-vacuum behaviour of the logarithmic mean than to local linear instabilities. Overall, our results suggest that local linear instabilities do not pose a practical obstacle to the use of high-order entropy-stable schemes.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that local linear instabilities in entropy-stable discretizations are not practically problematic, as they are dominated by oscillatory/boundary-localized modes, bounded, controlled by small dissipation, and do not extend to nonlinear flux discretizations. The real robustness issue lies in poor near-vacuum behavior of the logarithmic mean, not linear instabilities.
AI.motivation: Previous research has raised concerns about local linear instabilities in entropy-stable schemes, potentially questioning their practical robustness. This paper aims to clarify whether such instabilities truly threaten the use of high-order entropy-stable discretizations.
AI.method: The authors use modified-equation analysis, Floquet analysis, and numerical experiments on split-form discretizations of variable-coefficient linear advection equations (from Burgers linearizations) and nonlinear two-point-flux discretizations (Euler equations). They derive perturbation growth bounds using geometric and logarithmic fluxes.
AI.result: Local linear instabilities produce modal growth that is bounded, physically interpretable, typically small, dominated by high-frequency/boundary-localized modes, and controllable by minimal numerical dissipation. Unstable frozen-baseflow Jacobian spectra do not guarantee unstable perturbation growth in nonlinear settings. The logarithmic mean's near-vacuum behavior, not linear instabilities, causes robustness issues.
AI.conclusion: Local linear instabilities do not pose a practical obstacle to using high-order entropy-stable schemes; the primary robustness concern is poor near-vacuum performance of the logarithmic mean.

title: Multiscale computational approaches to magnetic behaviour in Cobalt Ferrite (CoFe$_2$O$_4$) nanostructures
summary: Cobalt ferrite (CoFe$_2$O$_4$) is a prototypical ferrimagnetic spinel oxide whose exceptional magnetic anisotropy, magnetoelastic coupling, and thermal stability underpin applications in spintronics, magnetic hyperthermia, energy harvesting, and catalysis. This chapter presents a comprehensive computational framework that integrates electronic$-$structure calculations with atomistic spin modeling, statistical mechanics, and continuum micromagnetics to predict magnetic functionality across length and time scales. Starting from density functional theory with Hubbard corrections (DFT$+$U), we derive exchange constants J$_{ij}$, magnetocrystalline anisotropy K$_1$, and magnetoelastic coefficients B$_1$, accounting for cation inversion, strain, and correlation effects. These parameters feed into generalized Heisenberg Hamiltonians, enabling Monte Carlo and Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert simulations of finite-size effects, hysteresis, coercivity, and hyperthermia response in nanoparticles and thin films. Coarse-graining strategies bridge to micromagnetic modeling, ensuring consistent parameter flow without empirical fitting. Computational case studies demonstrate size-dependent anisotropy enhancement, surface spin disorder, strain-tunable switching, and doping trends, revealing design principles inaccessible to experiment alone. Validation against benchmarks, e.g. Curie temperature, anisotropy constants, coercivity, magnetostriction, confirms predictive accuracy. Current challenges, e.g., U$-$parameter sensitivity, realistic surface chemistry, spin-lattice coupling, and large-scale integration are discussed alongside emerging directions including DFT$+$DMFT, coupled dynamics, and machine-learned potentials.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a multi-scale computational framework for CoFe₂O₄, integrating DFT+U, atomistic spin modeling, and micromagnetics to predict magnetic properties across scales, validated against experimental benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Cobalt ferrite has exceptional magnetic properties relevant to spintronics, hyperthermia, and energy harvesting, but predicting its behavior across different length and time scales remains challenging; the paper aims to bridge this gap with a unified computational framework.
AI.method: The framework integrates DFT+U for deriving exchange constants, magnetocrystalline anisotropy, and magnetoelastic coefficients; these parameters feed into Heisenberg Hamiltonians for Monte Carlo and LLG simulations, then coarse-grained to continuum micromagnetics.
AI.result: Case studies demonstrate size-dependent anisotropy enhancement, surface spin disorder, strain-tunable switching, and doping trends. Validation against Curie temperature, coercivity, and magnetostriction confirms predictive accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The multi-scale approach successfully predicts CoFe₂O₄ magnetic functionality without empirical fitting, though challenges remain in U-parameter sensitivity, surface chemistry, spin-lattice coupling, and computational scale-up.

title: Stability estimates for systems of nonlocal balance laws with memory
summary: In this work, we investigate entropy solutions for a class of systems of nonlocal {balance laws in which the convective flux and the source involves terms where the state variable convolved with kernels} in both spatial and temporal variables. This formulation captures the dependence of the flux on the solution within its spatial neighborhood (spatial nonlocality) as well as on its past states (temporal nonlocality), thereby incorporating memory effects. The resulting systems are coupled through these nonlocal interactions. We establish stability estimates for entropy solutions with respect to perturbations in the flux, the spatial and temporal kernels, and the initial data for the corresponding initial value problems. Finally, we present numerical experiments to illustrate the theoretical results and to highlight the influence of memory and source terms on the solution dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper studies entropy solutions for nonlocal balance law systems with spatial and temporal convolution in flux and source terms, establishes stability estimates, and presents numerical experiments.
AI.motivation: To extend the theory of entropy solutions to systems of balance laws that incorporate both spatial nonlocality (neighborhood dependence) and temporal nonlocality (memory effects) in the convective flux and source terms, which are coupled through nonlocal interactions.
AI.method: The authors formulate a class of nonlocal balance law systems where the convective flux and source involve convolutions of the state variable with spatial and temporal kernels. They derive stability estimates for entropy solutions under perturbations to the flux, kernels, and initial data, and support the analysis with numerical experiments.
AI.result: Stability estimates for entropy solutions are established with respect to perturbations in the flux, spatial/temporal kernels, and initial data. Numerical experiments validate the theoretical findings and demonstrate the impact of memory and source terms on solution dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully captures spatial and temporal nonlocal effects in balance laws, with rigorous stability guarantees. Memory effects and source terms significantly influence solution behavior, as confirmed numerically.

title: A Structure-Preserving Stagewise Rescaling Algorithm for a Two-Dimensional Nonlocal MEMS Equation in an Asymptotically Constant-Feedback Regime
summary:
Nonlocal MEMS equations exhibit finite-time quenching, or touchdown, which is difficult to capture numerically. We study a stagewise rescaling algorithm for a two-dimensional nonlocal MEMS equation in an asymptotically constant-feedback touchdown regime. The equation is not exactly invariant under the $A^{3/2}$--$A^3$ scaling used here; the scaling is justified when the reciprocal-integral feedback $K(t)=1+\int_Ω(1-u)^{-1}dx$ remains bounded and converges to a finite positive limit, as in the single-point touchdown profiles of Duong--Zaag. In this regime the leading-order core dynamics reduce to a local MEMS equation with an asymptotically constant coefficient.
  Using a fixed-stage scaling of the deficit variable, we obtain a gradient flow for a rescaled energy at frozen amplitude and prove an exact energy dissipation identity within each stage. We introduce a minimizing-movement stage solver and derive a discrete energy inequality. Since strict monotonicity need not hold across stage transitions, we separate the switch and outer-update defects and prove an exact defect balance. Under a uniform switch-defect estimate, this yields quantitative almost monotonicity and a defect-aware criterion for nonexistence of a global admissible continuation. The numerical section is organized around reproducible two-dimensional reference computations: a full-domain stagewise run showing trigger detection, fixed-stage energy decay, and geometric accumulation of physical time, and a direct fixed-domain energy check. These tests are not used as proof of the bounded-window criterion; instead, they report finite-feedback diagnostics and identify the ideal-transfer switch-energy diagnostics required for a posteriori verification.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a stagewise rescaling algorithm for 2D nonlocal MEMS equations in a touchdown regime, proving exact energy dissipation within stages and an exact defect balance across stage transitions, with numerical validation showing energy decay and trigger detection.
AI.motivation: Nonlocal MEMS equations exhibit finite-time quenching (touchdown) that is numerically difficult to capture, motivating the need for a robust rescaling algorithm.
AI.method: A fixed-stage scaling of the deficit variable is used, producing a gradient flow with exact energy dissipation per stage. A minimizing-movement stage solver is introduced, and defects across stage transitions are separated into switch and outer-update components, with an exact defect balance proven.
AI.result: Quantitative almost monotonicity and a defect-aware criterion for nonexistence of global admissible continuation are derived under a uniform switch-defect estimate. Numerical experiments (2D full-domain stagewise runs) demonstrate trigger detection, fixed-stage energy decay, and geometric accumulation of physical time.
AI.conclusion: The bounded-window criterion is not proven solely by numerical tests; the reported finite-feedback diagnostics and ideal-transfer switch-energy diagnostics provide a posteriori verification tools for the algorithm.

title: Accelerating Noisy Variational Quantum Algorithms with Physics-Informed Denoising Networks
summary: Variational quantum algorithms are promising for near-term quantum computing, but are severely limited by hardware noise and the substantial circuit overhead required for error mitigation methods such as Zero-Noise Extrapolation (ZNE). We propose a Physics-Informed Denoising Network (PIDN) that reduces the cost of ZNE by learning a surrogate model of its optimization dynamics. By viewing the variational update as a trajectory in the parameter space, PIDN is trained to reproduce ZNE-mitigated expectation values and gradient directions while incorporating a physics-informed loss that preserves the gradient descent dynamics. Once trained, PIDN replaces repeated multi-noise evaluations with denoised expectation and gradient estimation directly from the current noisy observation and the historical trajectory, significantly reducing circuit executions. We benchmark the approach on the quantum approximate optimization algorithm for 3-regular graphs, Sherrington-Kirkpatrick, and transverse-field Ising models, as well as the variational quantum eigensolver for LiH, BeH$_2$ and H$_2$O. Across all tasks, PIDN attains performance comparable to ZNE, while reducing the number of circuit executions by a factor of approximately 4 to 6. Gradient cosine similarity with ZNE remains above 0.95 throughout training. Robustness analysis shows that PIDN fails only when ZNE itself becomes unreliable, and ablation studies confirm the necessity of the physics-informed loss for maintaining directional consistency. We further find that PIDN tracks optimization dynamics most accurately when the effective loss landscape retains strong low-frequency structure. These results establish PIDN as a scalable, resource-efficient strategy for noise-resilient variational optimization in the noisy intermediate-scale quantum regime.

AI.tldr: PIDN is a physics-informed neural network that learns to replace repeated noisy circuit evaluations in Zero-Noise Extrapolation, achieving comparable performance while reducing circuit executions by 4-6x across QAOA and VQE benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Variational quantum algorithms are limited by hardware noise and the high circuit overhead of error mitigation methods like Zero-Noise Extrapolation (ZNE), which requires multiple noise-level evaluations per iteration.
AI.method: PIDN learns a surrogate model of ZNE optimization dynamics by treating variational updates as trajectories in parameter space. It is trained to reproduce ZNE-mitigated expectation values and gradients while incorporating a physics-informed loss to preserve gradient descent dynamics. After training, PIDN directly outputs denoised estimates from the current noisy observation and historical trajectory, eliminating repeated multi-noise circuit evaluations.
AI.result: PIDN achieves performance comparable to ZNE across QAOA (3-regular graphs, SK model, TFIM) and VQE (LiH, BeH₂, H₂O) benchmarks, reducing circuit executions by ~4-6x. Gradient cosine similarity with ZNE stays above 0.95. PIDN fails only when ZNE itself becomes unreliable, and the physics-informed loss is essential for directional consistency.
AI.conclusion: PIDN establishes a scalable, resource-efficient strategy for noise-resilient variational optimization in the NISQ regime, with best performance when the loss landscape retains strong low-frequency structure.

title: Global well-posedness for a one-dimensional quasilinear wave equation

AI.tldr: This paper proves global well-posedness for a specific one-dimensional quasilinear wave equation, partially resolving an open problem by Glassey, Hunter, and Zheng, using a new comparison principle for Riemann variables.
AI.motivation: To resolve an open problem posed by Glassey, Hunter, and Zheng regarding global existence of smooth solutions to the quasilinear wave equation for large initial data.
AI.method: Upper and lower estimates for Riemann variables via a new comparison principle.
AI.result: Global well-posedness for the one-dimensional quasilinear wave equation with positive, bounded, monotonically increasing c with bounded derivative.
AI.conclusion: The result provides a partial resolution to the open problem, with the method offering potential applicability to similar quasilinear wave equations.

title: A Finite Element Method for Elliptic Hemivariational Inequalities in Non-isotropic and Heterogeneous Semipermeable Media
summary: This work investigates finite element approximations for a general class of elliptic hemivariational inequalities arising in semipermeable media. The proposed model incorporates non-isotropic and heterogeneous diffusion coefficients, alongside both interior and boundary semipermeability terms, extending the isotropic and homogeneous framework examined by Han (2019). The existence and uniqueness of solutions are rigorously established. An optimal a priori error estimate for the linear finite element approximation is derived under appropriate solution regularity assumptions. Numerical experiments are presented to corroborate the theoretical analysis and to confirm the optimal convergence rates for the non-isotropic and heterogeneous case.

AI.tldr: This paper develops finite element approximations for elliptic hemivariational inequalities with non-isotropic and heterogeneous diffusion, proving existence, uniqueness, optimal error estimates, and validating results with numerical experiments.
AI.motivation: To extend prior work (Han, 2019) from isotropic/homogeneous settings to more realistic non-isotropic and heterogeneous diffusion scenarios in semipermeable media problems.
AI.method: The study employs finite element approximations for elliptic hemivariational inequalities, incorporating non-isotropic and heterogeneous diffusion coefficients with interior and boundary semipermeability terms. Rigorous existence/uniqueness proofs and optimal a priori error estimates for linear finite elements under regularity assumptions.
AI.result: Optimal a priori error estimates for linear finite element approximation are derived, and numerical experiments confirm optimal convergence rates for the non-isotropic and heterogeneous case.
AI.conclusion: The proposed finite element method for the generalized class of elliptic hemivariational inequalities achieves optimal convergence and is validated theoretically and numerically.

title: Composition-Weighted Symbolic Regression for General-Purpose Property Prediction
summary: We introduce a composition-weighted symbolic regression framework for interpretable prediction of materials properties directly from chemical composition. The method jointly learns analytical functional forms and task-dependent elemental weightings without predefined descriptors. By incorporating max/min operators, it naturally enforces constraints such as non-negative band gaps and bounded classification probabilities, unifying regression and classification tasks. Efficient search is achieved through a hybrid Monte Carlo tree search--genetic programming algorithm with gradient-based refinement and parallel computation. Benchmarks on MatBench tasks show competitive accuracy relative to state-of-the-art black-box models while yielding explicit analytical expressions. Applied to III--V semiconductor alloys, the model produces smooth composition-dependent trends and learned elemental weights with chemically meaningful periodic behavior. This framework provides a scalable and interpretable route for materials discovery and property screening.

AI.tldr: A composition-weighted symbolic regression framework that learns interpretable analytical expressions for materials properties directly from chemical compositions, achieving competitive accuracy with black-box models while providing explicit formulas and chemically meaningful elemental weights.
AI.motivation: Existing machine learning models for materials property prediction often act as black boxes, lacking interpretability. There is a need for methods that directly learn analytical functional forms from chemical compositions without predefined descriptors, enabling transparent and physically meaningful predictions.
AI.method: A composition-weighted symbolic regression framework that jointly learns analytical functional forms and task-dependent elemental weightings. It incorporates max/min operators for constraint enforcement (e.g., non-negative band gaps), and uses a hybrid Monte Carlo tree search-genetic programming algorithm with gradient-based refinement and parallel computation for efficient search.
AI.result: Competitive accuracy on MatBench tasks compared to state-of-the-art black-box models. Applied to III-V semiconductor alloys, the model produces smooth composition-dependent trends and learned elemental weights exhibiting chemically meaningful periodic behavior.
AI.conclusion: This framework offers a scalable and interpretable approach for materials discovery and property screening, bridging the gap between predictive accuracy and physical interpretability.

title: Large-Data Global Regularity for Three-Dimensional Navier--Stokes II: A Direct First-Threshold Continuation Proof for the Full System
summary: This is the second paper in a two-part direct-threshold series on large-data global regularity for the three-dimensional incompressible Navier--Stokes equations. It gives the full-system first-threshold continuation argument and uses the companion Part I theorem, which proves the large-data axisymmetric-with-swirl class by the direct full-Dirichlet method. The present paper treats the genuinely three-dimensional front end. A combined critical packet envelope is introduced, and the first time at which this envelope reaches a prescribed level is analyzed by finite-overlap packet selection. The proof uses angular Littlewood--Paley triads, finite-dimensional active-frame rigidity, passive-strain visibility, a quantitative zero-final-defect rigidity theorem, and the companion Part I axisymmetric direct theorem. The main finite-threshold mechanism is that any large leakage, shell, tail, source, passive, phase, or fragmentation error either produces a descendant packet with explicit score lower bound or becomes perturbative. The remaining coherent packet is contracted by the strict local estimates. Thus no large first-threshold packet can occur, and the critical envelope remains bounded on every finite time interval.

AI.tldr: This paper completes a two-part series proving large-data global regularity for 3D Navier-Stokes equations by establishing a first-threshold continuation argument for the genuinely 3D front end, using a critical packet envelope and various advanced analytical tools.
AI.motivation: To extend the regularity results for the 3D incompressible Navier-Stokes equations from the axisymmetric-with-swirl case (Part I) to the genuinely three-dimensional case, addressing the full-system large-data global regularity problem.
AI.method: Introduces a combined critical packet envelope, analyzes the first time it reaches a prescribed level via finite-overlap packet selection, and employs angular Littlewood-Paley triads, finite-dimensional active-frame rigidity, passive-strain visibility, and a quantitative zero-final-defect rigidity theorem.
AI.result: Any large error (leakage, shell, tail, source, passive, phase, or fragmentation) either produces a descendant packet with explicit score lower bound or becomes perturbative; the remaining coherent packet is contracted by strict local estimates, so no large first-threshold packet can occur.
AI.conclusion: The critical envelope remains bounded on every finite time interval, establishing the desired regularity result.

title: Structure-preserving LDG methods for linear and nonlinear transport equations with gradient noise
summary: We develop local discontinuous Galerkin (LDG) methods for conservation laws with heterogeneous stochastic fluxes, where the Stratonovich-driven transport terms may be linear or nonlinear. Such equations arise, for example, in simplified turbulence models, mean field games, and fluctuating hydrodynamics. Starting from the Itô formulation, we construct semi-discretizations that build the cancellation mechanism of transport noise into the numerical method. At the discrete energy level, the second-order Stratonovich-Itô correction is balanced by the quadratic variation, up to numerical flux terms, so that the hyperbolic stability structure is retained. Suitable numerical fluxes yield discrete energy conservation or energy dissipation, valid either pathwise or in expectation. The resulting high-order schemes are proved well posed through stability estimates combined with a Khasminskii-type argument, without imposing linear growth assumptions. Numerical experiments confirm stability and high-order accuracy.

AI.tldr: This paper develops LDG methods for conservation laws with stochastic fluxes, preserving discrete energy stability through built-in Stratonovich-Itô correction mechanisms, and proves well-posedness via Khasminskii-type arguments without linear growth assumptions.
AI.motivation: To construct high-order numerical methods that correctly handle transport noise in conservation laws arising from turbulence models, mean field games, and fluctuating hydrodynamics, where the cancellation mechanism of Stratonovich noise must be preserved at the discrete level.
AI.method: Starting from the Itô formulation, semi-discrete LDG schemes are designed to embed the Stratonovich-Itô correction into the numerical method, ensuring that quadratic variation balances the correction term at the discrete energy level through appropriate numerical flux choices.
AI.result: The schemes achieve discrete energy conservation/dissipation (pathwise or in expectation), well-posedness via stability estimates and Khasminskii-type arguments without linear growth assumptions, and confirmed high-order accuracy in numerical experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LDG methods successfully extend high-order discretizations to stochastic conservation laws with heterogeneous fluxes, preserving essential stochastic stability structures.

title: First-Principles Effective Mass in the Three-Dimensional Uniform Electron Gas
summary: The quasiparticle effective mass $m^*$ of the three-dimensional uniform electron gas (UEG) is a fundamental Fermi-liquid parameter whose value and density dependence have remained controversial for decades. Using renormalized perturbation theory with explicit counterterms, we determine $m^*$ in the metallic regime ($r_s \le 6$) from first principles by two complementary routes -- the self-energy and the forward-scattering four-point vertex via the $p$-wave spin-symmetric Landau parameter $F_1^s$ -- that agree within uncertainties at each density through sixth renormalized order. The resulting $m^*/m$ remains close to unity throughout the metallic regime, with a shallow non-monotonic density dependence -- a minimum near $r_s\approx 1$ followed by a gentle upturn -- reflecting the interplay of exchange and dynamical screening in the self-energy, and disfavoring strong monotonic suppression. This finding supports a physical picture for the metallic UEG in which dominant charge correlations are concentrated in nearly forward scattering and generate only a weak $F_1^s$ component.
