AI.tldr: Using renormalized perturbation theory, the authors compute the quasiparticle effective mass of the uniform electron gas in the metallic regime, finding it remains close to unity with a shallow non-monotonic density dependence, resolving a long-standing controversy.
AI.motivation: The quasiparticle effective mass m* of the three-dimensional uniform electron gas is a fundamental Fermi-liquid parameter whose value and density dependence have remained controversial for decades.
AI.method: Renormalized perturbation theory with explicit counterterms, computing m* via two complementary routes: the self-energy and the forward-scattering four-point vertex via the p-wave spin-symmetric Landau parameter F1^s, through sixth renormalized order.
AI.result: m*/m remains close to unity throughout the metallic regime (rs ≤ 6), with a shallow non-monotonic density dependence — a minimum near rs≈1 followed by a gentle upturn. The two complementary routes agree within uncertainties.
AI.conclusion: Strong monotonic suppression of m* is disfavored. The findings support a physical picture where dominant charge correlations are concentrated in nearly forward scattering and generate only a weak F1^s component.

title: Large-Data Global Regularity for Three-Dimensional Navier--Stokes I: A Direct First-Threshold Continuation Proof for the Axisymmetric Swirl Class
summary:
This is the first paper in a two-part direct-threshold series on large-data global regularity for the three-dimensional Navier--Stokes equations. We prove a direct first-threshold continuation theorem for the axisymmetric class with swirl. The proof is written entirely in the lifted variables \[
  Γ=ru_θ,\qquad G=ω_θ/r,\qquad dμ_5=r^3\,dr\,dz, \] and uses the five-dimensional full-Dirichlet visibility \(\mathcal V_χ\) as the local coercive quantity. The argument is organized by a finite first-threshold stopping time. We define a critical axis score envelope, follow it to a first possible threshold time, and prove that the corresponding normalized packet cannot exist. The proof has three quantitative ingredients. First, a small-envelope continuation theorem converts bounded score and regularized source size into smooth continuation. Second, a finite-overlap descendant-extraction theorem shows that every large collar leakage, exterior tail, low-frequency residue, source concentration, or fragmentation channel either produces a smaller descendant packet or is perturbative. Third, in the remaining coherent case, the strict full-Dirichlet bridge \[
  |\mathcal T_{G,χ}[G]|
  \le θ\mathcal V_χ[G]+C\mathfrak E_{\rm dir}[G],
  \qquad 0<θ<1, \] and a coefficient-calibrated local balance contract the selected packet. Consequently no first threshold occurs, the critical envelope stays finite on every bounded time interval, and the classical continuation criterion gives global smoothness. The local mechanisms used by the direct continuation proof--the strict bridge, the source-to-score conversion, the finite-overlap extraction, and the finite-threshold contraction--are stated in the precise forms used in the argument and proved in the text.

AI.tldr: This paper proves a direct first-threshold continuation theorem for axisymmetric 3D Navier-Stokes equations with swirl, using lifted variables and a five-dimensional full-Dirichlet visibility as the coercive quantity, establishing global regularity.
AI.motivation: To address the global regularity problem for the 3D Navier-Stokes equations in the axisymmetric swirl case by establishing a direct first-threshold continuation theorem that prevents finite-time singularity formation.
AI.method: The proof uses lifted variables (Γ=ru_θ, G=ω_θ/r, dμ_5=r^3 dr dz), a 5D full-Dirichlet visibility V_χ as the local coercive quantity, a finite first-threshold stopping time, and a critical axis score envelope. Three quantitative ingredients: (1) small-envelope continuation theorem, (2) finite-overlap descendant-extraction theorem, (3) strict full-Dirichlet bridge with coefficient-calibrated local balance.
AI.result: No first threshold occurs; the critical envelope stays finite on every bounded time interval; the classical continuation criterion yields global smoothness.
AI.conclusion: The direct continuation proof establishes global regularity for axisymmetric Navier-Stokes equations with swirl, with precise statements of the four local mechanisms used.

title: Well-Balanced Subcell Limiting for Discontinuous Galerkin Discretizations of the Shallow-Water Equations
summary:
High-order discontinuous Galerkin (DG) methods equipped with subcell finite-volume (FV) limiters provide an efficient framework for the simulation of nonlinear hyperbolic balance laws featuring shocks and complex flow structures. However, for systems with non-conservative terms, the design of hybrid DG/FV schemes that simultaneously guarantee high-order accuracy, robustness, and well-balancedness remains challenging. In particular, for the shallow water equations with variable bottom topography, standard flux-differencing formulations combined with node-wise subcell limiting generally destroy the well-balanced property, even if both the underlying DG and FV methods are individually well-balanced.
  In this work, we propose a novel flux-differencing formulation for non-conservative systems that enables node-wise subcell limiting while preserving steady states exactly. The key idea is to construct staggered DG fluxes whose non-conservative contributions are in local-times-jump form and vanish individually at equilibrium. To achieve this, we introduce a reformulation of the shallow water equations in which the source term is proportional to the gradient of the total water height. This reformulation allows the design of staggered fluxes that preserve equilibrium locally at the node level, thereby enabling arbitrary nodal blending with low-order FV fluxes.
  The resulting DG/FV method is high-order accurate, robust, and exactly well-balanced under node-wise limiting. Numerical experiments, including two-dimensional dam-break configurations with wet/dry fronts and complex obstacle interactions, demonstrate the improved stability and accuracy of the proposed approach.
  Although this work focuses on the shallow water equations, the well-balanced hybrid DG/FV methods developed here are applicable to a broader class of nonlinear systems of balance laws.

AI.tldr: A novel flux-differencing formulation for non-conservative systems enables node-wise subcell limiting while preserving exact well-balancedness in high-order hybrid DG/FV schemes for shallow water equations with variable bottom topography.
AI.motivation: Standard flux-differencing formulations combined with node-wise subcell limiting destroy the well-balanced property of hybrid DG/FV schemes for non-conservative systems like shallow water equations with variable bottom topography, even when both the underlying DG and FV methods are individually well-balanced.
AI.method: The authors reformulate the shallow water equations so the source term is proportional to the gradient of total water height, then construct staggered DG fluxes with non-conservative contributions in local-times-jump form that vanish individually at equilibrium, enabling arbitrary nodal blending with low-order FV fluxes while preserving well-balancedness.
AI.result: The method achieves high-order accuracy, robustness, and exact well-balancedness under node-wise limiting. Numerical experiments (2D dam-break with wet/dry fronts and complex obstacles) demonstrate improved stability and accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed well-balanced hybrid DG/FV framework extends beyond shallow water equations to a broader class of nonlinear balance laws, providing a generalizable solution for maintaining equilibrium states under subcell limiting.

title: Designing explicit functionals for the charge density in terms of a potential
summary: One of the most powerful strategies to address properties of real many-body systems is to incorporate data obtained for models, for example, to use data of the homogeneous electron gas in order to build the Local Density Approximation for the Kohn-Sham exchange-correlation potential. In the present work, we examine to what extent we can use model data to design functionals directly for observables of materials. In particular, we study different approximations for the charge density of real inhomogeneous materials expressed as a simple, explicit functional of a given Kohn-Sham potential, using as central building block the Lindhard density-density response function of the homogeneous electron gas. Our increasingly realistic set of approximations includes a fully nearsighted expression equivalent to the Thomas-Fermi approximation, functional Taylor expansions, and different approximations to the Connector Theory developed in [Aouina \textit{et al.}, npj Computational Materials {\bf 11}, 242 (2025)]. In all cases, the charge density is obtained without ever solving the Kohn-Sham Schrödinger equation. Results for cubic helium, a prototypical strongly inhomogeneous material, systematically improve with higher levels of approximation, indicating that this is a promising route to obtain expressions that are relatively simple to calculate and to analyze.

AI.tldr: This paper explores whether model data (like the homogeneous electron gas) can be used to design functionals for material observables directly, without solving the Kohn-Sham equation. They test various approximations for charge density—from Thomas-Fermi to Connector Theory—on cubic helium, showing systematic improvement with higher-level approximations.
AI.motivation: To examine whether model data (e.g., from homogeneous electron gas) can be used to construct explicit functionals for charge density of real inhomogeneous materials, bypassing the need to solve the Kohn-Sham equation.
AI.method: The authors test a hierarchy of approximations for charge density as a functional of the Kohn-Sham potential, using the Lindhard response function as a building block. This includes: nearsighted (Thomas-Fermi), functional Taylor expansions, and Connector Theory approximations.
AI.result: Results for cubic helium show systematic improvement as higher-level approximations are used, indicating that this route is promising for obtaining simple, analyzable expressions.
AI.conclusion: Using homogeneous electron gas data to directly construct density functionals (without solving Schrödinger equation) is a promising approach, with approximation quality improving systematically.

title: Renormalized Solution for the Nonlinear Parabolic Problem with Lower Order Terms
summary:
In this paper, we consider the following nonlinear parabolic equation with non-coercive terms in \(R^N\) space \[ \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} -
abla \cdot (a(x,t,u,
abla u)+ Φ(x,t,
abla u))=f, 	ext{ in }Ω	imes (0,T). \] Here \(Ω\) is a bounded open set of \(R^N\) with the boundary \(\partial Ω\) satisfying Lipschitz condition. The Carathéodory function \(Φ\) is restricted by $|Φ(x,t,s)|\le c(x,t)|s|^γ$ with parameters depending on $p$ and $N$. And the initial value $u(x,0)=u_0(x)$. For convenience, we define the domain $Q := Ω	imes (0,T)$ and the boundary similarly. Then for $f\in L^1(Q)$ and $u_0\in L^1(Ω)$, we prove the existence and uniqueness of a renormalized solution via truncation methods, monotone operator theory, and a prior gradient estimates.

AI.tldr: The paper proves existence and uniqueness of renormalized solutions for a nonlinear parabolic equation with non-coercive terms in bounded Lipschitz domains, using truncation methods, monotone operator theory, and gradient estimates.
AI.motivation: To address nonlinear parabolic equations with non-coercive terms in \(R^N\) space, where standard solution theories may fail due to the non-coercive nature of the \(\Phi\) term and low-regularity data (\(f \in L^1, u_0 \in L^1\)).
AI.method: Truncation methods, monotone operator theory, and a priori gradient estimates to handle the non-coercive Carathéodory function \(\Phi\) bounded by \(|Φ(x,t,s)|\le c(x,t)|s|^γ\).
AI.result: Existence and uniqueness of a renormalized solution for the nonlinear parabolic equation with \(f \in L^1(Q)\) and \(u_0 \in L^1(\Omega)\).
AI.conclusion: The renormalized solution framework effectively handles low-regularity data and non-coercive perturbations, extending existence/uniqueness results beyond classical settings.

title: Efficient generation of Gaussian random fields on metric graphs via domain decomposition and mass matrix lumping
summary: We consider Gaussian Random Fields on metric graphs defined implicitly as the stationary solution to a fractional SPDE driven by Gaussian white noise. Sampling from the finite element approximation requires the Cholesky factorization of the mass matrix, causing non-linear execution time explosions and massive memory fill-in on large graphs. Hence, we combine Neumann-Neumann graph decomposition with mass matrix lumping and demonstrate empirically, that our approach preserves exact theoretical convergence rates established in [8] while achieving multi-order speedups and massive memory reductions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a scalable approach for sampling Gaussian Random Fields on metric graphs by combining Neumann-Neumann graph decomposition with mass matrix lumping, achieving multi-order speedups and massive memory reductions while preserving theoretical convergence rates.
AI.motivation: Cholesky factorization of the mass matrix in finite element approximations causes non-linear execution time explosions and massive memory fill-in on large graphs, making sampling from Gaussian Random Fields computationally prohibitive.
AI.method: Combines Neumann-Neumann graph decomposition with mass matrix lumping to avoid the expensive Cholesky factorization step while maintaining accuracy.
AI.result: Achieves multi-order speedups and massive memory reductions while preserving exact theoretical convergence rates established in prior work [8].
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach offers an efficient and scalable alternative for sampling Gaussian Random Fields on large metric graphs without sacrificing theoretical convergence guarantees.

title: A sharp hypocoercive entropy decay estimate for underdamped Langevin dynamics

AI.tldr: The paper proves explicit exponential entropy decay for underdamped Langevin dynamics with optimal √ρ rate using a modified entropy with Wasserstein entropy-current corrector, under convex U satisfying LSI.
AI.motivation: To establish explicit and optimal convergence rates for underdamped Langevin dynamics, which is crucial for understanding sampling algorithms and non-equilibrium statistical mechanics.
AI.method: Introduces a modified entropy functional combining standard relative entropy with a Wasserstein entropy-current corrector term, then analyzes its decay using the logarithmic Sobolev inequality (ρ>0) and convexity of U.
AI.result: Proves explicit exponential decay bound: Ent(p_t|μ) ≤ (1+θ)/(1-θ) e^{-Λt} Ent(p_0|μ) with rate Λ = θ/(2(1+θ))√ρ, where θ = min{Γ/12, 1/(4Γ)} and Γ = γ/√ρ.
AI.conclusion: The entropy convergence rate achieves optimal √ρ order, matching theoretical lower bounds for underdamped Langevin dynamics.

title: Parameter estimation for evaporation-driven tear film model in two space dimensions
summary: The tear film (TF) plays a critical role in maintaining ocular surface health, and its disruption through tear breakup (TBU) is closely associated with dry eye disease. Evaporation-driven thinning is a primary mechanism underlying TBU, yet quantitative in vivo estimates of key physical parameters remain limited. In this work, we fit an evaporation-driven TF thinning model, originally developed by Braun et al. and extended to two dimensions using proper orthogonal decomposition (POD) by Chen et al., to experimental fluorescence (FL) imaging data from normal subjects. The use of dimension reduction enables efficient solution of the governing PDEs and facilitates parameter estimation from imaging data. Our results provide in vivo estimates of evaporation-related and thinning parameters within TBU regions. These findings enhance understanding of TF thinning and dry-spot formation and establish a quantitative baseline for comparison with dry eye patient data.

AI.tldr: This paper uses a reduced-order model (POD-based) to fit evaporation-driven tear film thinning to experimental fluorescence imaging data, providing in vivo estimates of key physical parameters in tear breakup regions from normal subjects.
AI.motivation: Quantitative in vivo estimates of key physical parameters underlying evaporation-driven tear breakup remain limited, hindering understanding of dry eye disease.
AI.method: A two-dimensional evaporation-driven TF thinning model (Braun et al., extended via POD by Chen et al.) is fitted to experimental fluorescence imaging data from normal subjects. Dimension reduction enables efficient PDE solving and parameter estimation.
AI.result: In vivo estimates of evaporation-related and thinning parameters within tear breakup regions are obtained.
AI.conclusion: The findings enhance understanding of TF thinning and dry-spot formation, and establish a quantitative baseline for comparison with dry eye patient data.

title: Polymer Knots in Thin Films: Thickness Dependence, Local Effects, and Stiffness
summary: We study how confinement affects topology and conformations in polymer films of varying thickness $h$. The knotting probability exhibits a maximum at intermediate thicknesses near the bulk radius of gyration $h \approx R_\mathrm{g,bulk}$, vanishes at small $h$ and approaches bulk values for large $h$. Close to walls, the entanglement length increases monotonically and conformations become flatter. A layer-resolved analysis of structural and topological properties allows us to reconstruct the explicit thickness dependencies by integrating layer-resolved properties of a thick film.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how confinement affects topology and conformations in polymer films of varying thickness, finding that knotting probability peaks at intermediate thicknesses near the bulk radius of gyration, while entanglement length increases near walls and conformations become flatter. Layer-resolved analysis explains thickness dependencies.
AI.motivation: To understand how confinement (film thickness) influences topological properties like knotting probability and entanglement length in polymer films, which has implications for polymer physics and materials science.
AI.method: Computational study of polymer films of varying thickness h, with layer-resolved analysis of structural and topological properties, comparing to bulk values.
AI.result: Knotting probability peaks at intermediate thickness h ≈ Rg,bulk, vanishes at small h, approaches bulk at large h. Entanglement length increases monotonically near walls. Conformations become flatter near walls.
AI.conclusion: Layer-resolved properties from thick films can reconstruct explicit thickness dependencies, providing a predictive framework for confinement effects on polymer topology.

title: Sets of finite perimeter on Riemannian manifolds and stochastic completeness
summary: We prove a heat semigroup characterization of the total variation for compactly supported ${\rm BV}$ on arbitrary smooth complete weighted Riemannian manifolds, extending the main result in \cite{GP15}. We then provide an example of a weighted manifold where such equivalence does not hold for a large class of sets of finite perimeter.

AI.tldr: The paper extends a heat semigroup characterization of total variation for compactly supported BV functions on weighted Riemannian manifolds, but shows the equivalence fails for a large class of sets of finite perimeter on some weighted manifolds.
AI.motivation: To generalize the heat semigroup characterization of total variation from the previous work (GP15) to arbitrary smooth complete weighted Riemannian manifolds, and to investigate the limitations of this characterization.
AI.method: The authors prove a heat semigroup characterization for compactly supported BV functions on weighted Riemannian manifolds, and then construct a counterexample on a specific weighted manifold to demonstrate failure for general sets of finite perimeter.
AI.result: The heat semigroup characterization holds for compactly supported BV functions, but it fails for a large class of sets of finite perimeter on some weighted manifolds.
AI.conclusion: The characterization works for compactly supported BV functions in general weighted manifolds, but the equivalence is not universal for all sets of finite perimeter, revealing a limitation of the approach.

title: Two-scale Neural Networks for Singularly Perturbed Dynamical Systems with Multiple Parameters
summary: We extend our two-scale neural-network method for scalar singularly perturbed problems with one small parameter to dynamical systems with multiple small parameters. To accommodate multiple small parameters, we use a single effective scale parameter defined as the geometric mean of all parameters. We thus augment the network input with a scale-aware feature, enabling it to capture sharp solution transitions intrinsically. Numerical experiments across a range of dynamical systems demonstrate that the proposed framework can handle coupled systems with multiple and high-contrast small parameters and obtain satisfactory accuracy in capturing solution features induced by small parameters.

AI.tldr: The authors extend their two-scale neural-network method from scalar singularly perturbed problems to dynamical systems with multiple small parameters, using the geometric mean as a single effective scale parameter, achieving satisfactory accuracy across various coupled systems.
AI.motivation: To extend existing two-scale neural-network methods (designed for scalar problems with one small parameter) to handle dynamical systems that involve multiple small parameters simultaneously.
AI.method: The method defines a single effective scale parameter as the geometric mean of all small parameters, and augments neural network input with a scale-aware feature to capture sharp solution transitions.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show the framework can handle coupled systems with multiple and high-contrast small parameters, obtaining satisfactory accuracy in capturing solution features induced by small parameters.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully generalizes the two-scale neural-network approach to multi-parameter dynamical systems, effectively capturing sharp transitions caused by multiple small parameters.

title: Existence and multiplicity of solutions for a critical Grushin problem with a singular nonlinearity
summary:
We investigate the existence and multiplicity of positive solutions to the problem \begin{equation} \begin{cases} \begin{aligned}
  - Δ_γ u &= λu^{p} + u^{-δ} &\quad \text{in } Ω,
  \quad u &= 0 &\quad \text{on } \partial Ω, \end{aligned} \end{cases} \end{equation} where $Δ_γ$ denotes the Grushin operator defined by \begin{equation} Δ_γ := Δ_x + (1+γ)^2 |x|^{2γ}Δ_y, \end{equation} with $γ>0$, $z=(x,y)\in \mathbb{R}^N$, $N=n+m$, $n \geq 1$, $m\geq 1$, $Ω\subset \mathbb{R}^N$ a smooth bounded domain, $λ>0$, $1<p<\infty$, and $δ>0$.
  The analysis depends on the exponent $p$, which may be subcritical, critical, or supercritical, that is, $p<2_γ^*-1$, $p=2_γ^*-1$, or $p>2_γ^*-1$, respectively, where $2_γ^*=\frac{2Q}{Q-2}$ is the critical Sobolev exponent associated with the Grushin operator, and $Q=m+(1+γ)n$ is the corresponding homogeneous dimension.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the existence and multiplicity of positive solutions for a semilinear elliptic problem involving the Grushin operator with a singular term and a source term, analyzing subcritical, critical, and supercritical regimes.
AI.motivation: To understand how the interplay between a singular nonlinearity ($u^{-\delta}$) and a power nonlinearity ($\lambda u^p$) affects the existence and number of positive solutions in the context of the degenerate Grushin operator, across different criticality regimes.
AI.method: Variational methods and critical point theory are employed, adapting Sobolev-type inequalities and compactness arguments to the Grushin operator's homogeneous dimension $Q$ and its critical exponent $2_\gamma^*$.
AI.result: The existence and multiplicity of positive solutions depend on the exponent $p$ relative to the critical Sobolev exponent $2_\gamma^*-1$: subcritical ($p<2_\gamma^*-1$), critical ($p=2_\gamma^*-1$), or supercritical ($p>2_\gamma^*-1$).
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a complete classification of solution behavior for the singular Grushin problem, showing that positive solutions exist under different conditions depending on whether the power exponent is subcritical, critical, or supercritical.

title: Factorization and monotonicity methods for reconstructing impenetrable obstacles in inverse biharmonic scattering
summary: The inverse scattering problem for biharmonic waves, governing flexural vibrations of elastic plates, presents fundamental analytical challenges distinct from acoustic inverse problems due to the fourth-order differential operator and higher-order boundary conditions. This paper addresses the reconstruction of impenetrable obstacles with Dirichlet or Neumann boundary conditions from far-field measurements. We establish new factorizations of the far-field operator by considering structures of the biharmonic fundamental solution and the boundary conditions. We rigorously prove that the factorizations satisfy the range identities and derive characterizations of the obstacle's support by the factorization methods, valid for all wavenumbers except the associated transmission eigenvalues. Furthermore, we establish a monotonicity relation for the eigenvalues of the far-field operator, which yields an alternative characterization of the obstacle's support that remains applicable for all wavenumbers. Numerical experiments for the Dirichlet obstacles with various shapes are presented to demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed reconstruction schemes.

AI.tldr: This paper develops factorization methods to reconstruct impenetrable obstacles in biharmonic wave scattering (elastic plates) from far-field data, proving range identities and monotonicity relations valid for all wavenumbers except transmission eigenvalues, with numerical validation.
AI.motivation: The inverse scattering problem for biharmonic waves governing flexural vibrations of elastic plates presents fundamental analytical challenges distinct from acoustic inverse problems due to the fourth-order differential operator and higher-order boundary conditions.
AI.method: New factorizations of the far-field operator are established by considering structures of the biharmonic fundamental solution and boundary conditions. The range identities are rigorously proved, and characterizations of obstacle support via factorization methods are derived. A monotonicity relation for eigenvalues of the far-field operator is also established.
AI.result: Factorization methods valid for all wavenumbers except associated transmission eigenvalues are proved. A monotonicity-based characterization works for all wavenumbers. Numerical experiments with Dirichlet obstacles of various shapes demonstrate effectiveness and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed reconstruction schemes effectively solve the inverse scattering problem for biharmonic waves with Dirichlet/Neumann boundary conditions, with the monotonicity approach offering applicability across all wavenumbers.

title: Structures of Identical Particle Systems : Efficient Computation of Many-Body Density of States
summary: We present a method for approximating the many-body density of states of a system of quantum identical particles, with a reduction of the computational cost by a combinatorial factor compared to the full calculation. This is carried out by considering an isolated quantum system of identical particles, and studying its non-interacting many-body spectrum through the use of a new approach based on a separation of universal combinatorial properties from the system-specific quantities. In this paper we focus on a practical computation method that leverages our formalism of many-body combinatorics, in order to perform an efficient numerical computation of the many-body density of states. In addition, this method provides further computational improvements by allowing most of the results to be cached in persistent storage and computed incrementally, making way for efficient use of parallelization and dynamic programming techniques. We give an extensive description of the method and provide several detailed examples of approximations of bosonic many-body density of states with tunable accuracy requirements. Lastly, we demonstrate how one such approximation can be used to recover Bose-Einstein-like distributions without any particle statistics assumptions.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a computationally efficient method for approximating the many-body density of states for quantum identical particles, using combinatorial techniques to reduce computational cost and enabling parallelization, with applications to bosonic systems and Bose-Einstein distributions.
AI.motivation: To reduce the computational cost of calculating the many-body density of states for identical quantum particles, which is combinatorially expensive when done exactly.
AI.method: A formalism of many-body combinatorics that separates universal combinatorial properties from system-specific quantities, enabling caching, incremental computation, parallelization, and dynamic programming.
AI.result: Demonstrated approximations of bosonic many-body density of states with tunable accuracy, and recovery of Bose-Einstein-like distributions without explicit particle statistics assumptions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method significantly reduces computational cost and enables efficient, scalable computation of many-body density of states for identical particles.

title: Serrin's overdetermined problem and sharp harmonic quadrature identities in the plane
summary: We study a weak formulation of Serrin's overdetermined boundary value problem in planar Jordan domains with rectifiable boundary. Our first result establishes that, within the class of rectifiable Jordan Smirnov domains, the corresponding harmonic quadrature identity, equivalent to Serrin's overdetermined problem, necessarily implies that the domain is a disk. Subsequently, we construct a family of rectifiable, non-Smirnov Jordan domains that nonetheless satisfy the same quadrature identity, thereby demonstrating the sharpness of the Smirnov regularity assumption. Consequently, there exists a nontrivial Jordan domain with rectifiable boundary satisfying the weak formulation of Serrin's overdetermined system in $\mathbb R^2$.

AI.tldr: This paper studies a weak version of Serrin's overdetermined boundary problem in 2D planar domains with rectifiable boundaries. It proves that under Smirnov regularity, the quadrature identity implies the domain must be a disk. However, it constructs examples of non-Smirnov rectifiable domains that still satisfy the identity, showing the Smirnov condition is sharp and that nontrivial rectifiable Jordan domains can satisfy the weak overdetermined problem.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether Serrin's overdetermined boundary value problem (which classically forces the domain to be a disk) can admit non-circular solutions when formulated weakly in planar domains with only rectifiable (not smooth) boundaries, and to understand the precise regularity threshold where the rigidity breaks down.
AI.method: The paper employs a weak formulation of Serrin's problem via harmonic quadrature identities in Jordan domains. It analyzes two cases: (1) rectifiable Jordan Smirnov domains, where it proves the identity forces the domain to be a disk; (2) rectifiable non-Smirnov domains, where it constructs explicit counterexamples satisfying the same quadrature identity.
AI.result: Main results: (i) For rectifiable Jordan Smirnov domains, the harmonic quadrature identity (equivalent to Serrin's overdetermined problem) implies the domain is a disk. (ii) There exist rectifiable, non-Smirnov Jordan domains that satisfy the same quadrature identity, demonstrating that the Smirnov assumption is necessary for the rigidity result.
AI.conclusion: The Smirnov regularity condition is sharp: below that threshold, nontrivial non-circular domains can satisfy the weak Serrin overdetermined problem. This provides a complete characterization of the regularity boundary between rigidity and flexibility in this problem.

title: Existence, Asymptotic Behavior, and Numerical Analysis of a Generalized Abel Differential Equation with Applications in Financial Modeling
summary: We present a comprehensive investigation into a generalized class of Abel ordinary differential equations (ODEs), extending the classical cubic form to arbitrary polynomial nonlinearities of degree $n \geq 1$. This work provides a rigorous treatment of the existence and uniqueness of regular solutions on both bounded and unbounded domains. Utilizing a unified barrier-based approach, we derive sharp growth rates and prove the existence of exact asymptotic plateaus, establishing the first systematic treatment of such generalizations in the literature. The regularity of solutions is rigorously justified through the lens of Sobolev spaces and classical ODE theory. To complement our analytical findings, we implement a high-order numerical framework based on Radau IIA implicit Runge--Kutta schemes, providing detailed stability arguments and error analysis. The numerical results demonstrate exceptional consistency with our theoretical predictions, particularly in capturing the asymptotic behavior. Finally, we discuss the implications of our results for real-world models, including Merton-type credit risk analysis and Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman stochastic control problems, bridging the gap between abstract nonlinear dynamics and applied science.

AI.tldr: This paper generalizes Abel ODEs to arbitrary polynomial nonlinearities, proves existence/uniqueness of solutions, derives asymptotic plateaus via barrier methods, validates results with high-order Radau IIA numerical schemes, and applies findings to credit risk and stochastic control.
AI.motivation: To generalize classical cubic Abel ODEs to arbitrary polynomial nonlinearities of degree n≥1, providing the first systematic theoretical treatment and bridging the gap to real-world applications.
AI.method: Unified barrier-based approach for existence, uniqueness, and asymptotic analysis; Sobolev space and classical ODE theory for regularity; high-order Radau IIA implicit Runge–Kutta schemes for numerical validation.
AI.result: Sharp growth rates and exact asymptotic plateaus proven; numerical results show exceptional consistency with theoretical predictions, especially in asymptotic behavior capture.
AI.conclusion: The generalized Abel ODE framework is rigorously established and validated; implications for Merton-type credit risk and Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman stochastic control problems demonstrate practical relevance.

title: Quantitative Estimates for Mean-Field Limits and Correlation Functions through a Duality Framework
summary: We investigate the mean-field limit for interacting particle systems through a duality-based framework and obtain quantitative estimates on the convergence of marginals as well as on correlation functions. In particular, for merely square-integrable interaction forces, we derive the natural fluctuation-scale rate $\mathcal{O}(N^{-1/2})$. By introducing an iterative argument on the hierarchy of dual cumulants, we leverage this bound to recover the optimal mean-field rate $\mathcal{O}(N^{-1})$ and to obtain robust estimates on the dual cumulants, at the expense of corresponding regularity assumptions on the interaction kernel. Finally, using the relation between dual and direct correlations, we transfer these bounds to direct cumulants, yielding refined information on correlations and deviations from chaos.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a duality-based framework for mean-field limits of interacting particle systems, achieving optimal convergence rates ($O(N^{-1/2})$ for square-integrable forces and $O(N^{-1})$ under regularity assumptions) by iterating on dual cumulant hierarchies, and translating these results to direct cumulants for correlation analysis.
AI.motivation: To obtain quantitative convergence estimates for mean-field limits of interacting particle systems, particularly achieving optimal rates under minimal regularity assumptions on interaction forces, and to understand correlation structures beyond chaos.
AI.method: Uses a duality-based framework to analyze the hierarchy of dual cumulants, employs iterative arguments to improve convergence rates, and leverages relationships between dual and direct correlations to transfer bounds.
AI.result: Derives $O(N^{-1/2})$ convergence rate for square-integrable interaction forces (fluctuation scale), and recovers optimal $O(N^{-1})$ rate under regularity assumptions via iterative dual cumulant arguments. Also obtains refined estimates on direct cumulants.
AI.conclusion: The duality-based approach provides a systematic way to obtain quantitative mean-field estimates at both fluctuation and optimal scales, with robust control over correlations and deviations from chaos.

title: Modelling the electrophysiological interactions between human pluripotent cell-derived cardiomyocite grafts and host ventricular tissue
comment: 23 pages, 10 figures, as submitted to PLOS Comp Biol
summary: Human pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes (hPSC-CMs) are a promising therapy for regenerating myocardium after infarction, but their use is limited by graft-related arrhythmias that frequently occur shortly after transplantation. Experimental studies indicate that these arrhythmias can originate within the graft, which may act as an ectopic pacemaker, yet the mechanisms governing successful excitation of host tissue remain poorly understood. In particular, the role of electrical coupling at the graft-host interface is important, but difficult to measure directly or control. Computer modelling can help here. Here, we present a computational framework that enables systematic investigation of graft-host electrical interactions using a physiologically interpretable parameterisation. We model the graft-host interface as an internal boundary with a defined specific conductance, allowing direct control over coupling strength in units that correspond to measurable tissue properties. We formulate the governing equations and implement the computations using both finite-difference and finite-element discretisations in established cardiac modelling platforms. Using representative anatomical and physiological configurations, we demonstrate how variations in interface conductance influence the ability of spontaneous graft activity to initiate propagating excitation in host tissue. This framework provides a reproducible, mechanistically transparent tool for studying graft-related arrhythmogenesis and lays a foundation for evaluating strategies to mitigate arrhythmic risk in cardiac cell therapy.

AI.tldr: A computational framework modeling electrical coupling at the graft-host interface in hPSC-CM transplantation, showing how interface conductance influences arrhythmia initiation.
AI.motivation: Graft-related arrhythmias limit the therapeutic use of hPSC-CMs for myocardial regeneration, but the mechanisms of graft-host electrical coupling and excitation propagation are poorly understood and difficult to study experimentally.
AI.method: A computational framework parameterizing the graft-host interface as an internal boundary with defined specific conductance, implemented using finite-difference and finite-element discretizations in cardiac modeling platforms.
AI.result: Variations in interface conductance directly influence the ability of spontaneous graft activity to initiate propagating excitation in host tissue.
AI.conclusion: This reproducible, mechanistically transparent tool enables systematic study of graft-related arrhythmogenesis and supports development of strategies to mitigate arrhythmic risk in cardiac cell therapy.

title: Volumetric density estimates for nonlocal minimal surfaces
summary: In this article, we prove that viscosity subsolutions to nonlocal mean curvature-type equations satisfy universal volumetric estimates at all scales. Our results hold for general symmetric kernels that are comparable to the fractional Laplacian. Furthermore, we prove that subsolutions with low density (with respect to a universal constant) necessarily have `fat boundary', that is, have topological boundary with positive Lebesgue measure.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that viscosity subsolutions to nonlocal mean curvature-type equations satisfy universal volumetric estimates and that low-density subsolutions necessarily have boundaries with positive Lebesgue measure.
AI.motivation: To establish universal volumetric estimates for viscosity subsolutions to nonlocal mean curvature-type equations with general symmetric kernels comparable to the fractional Laplacian, and to link low subsolution density to the existence of "fat boundaries" with positive measure.
AI.method: The paper uses analytic techniques to prove volumetric estimates for viscosity subsolutions under general symmetric kernel assumptions comparable to the fractional Laplacian.
AI.result: (1) Universal volumetric estimates at all scales hold for viscosity subsolutions. (2) Subsolutions with low density (relative to a universal constant) have topological boundaries with positive Lebesgue measure.
AI.conclusion: The work provides fundamental regularity results for nonlocal mean curvature-type equations, showing that subsolutions with low density must possess "fat" boundaries.

title: Quaternion Nonlinear Transform-Induced Nuclear Norm for Low-Rank Tensor Completion
summary: Tensor completion has emerged as a powerful framework for recovering missing data in multidimensional signals by exploiting low-rank tensor structures. Among existing approaches, linear transform-based tensor nuclear norm (TNN) methods have achieved considerable success by enforcing low-rankness on transformed frontal slices. However, the low-rank structure revealed by linear transforms remains inherently limited. To better capture intrinsic correlations, nonlinear transform-based TNN (NTTNN) models have been proposed, significantly enhancing low-rank representation through composite transforms. Despite their effectiveness, existing NTTNN methods are restricted to real-valued tensors and fail to model quaternion-valued data, which are essential for preserving inter-channel dependencies in color images and videos. Extending nonlinear TNN models to the quaternion domain is challenging due to the non-commutativity of quaternion multiplication and the complexity of quaternion singular value decomposition. To address the limitations encountered in prior works, we propose a quaternion nonlinear transform-induced tensor nuclear norm (QNTTNN) via a real embedding of quaternions, enabling tractable nuclear norm definitions and efficient optimization. Building upon QNTTNN, we formulate a quaternion tensor completion model and develop a proximal alternating minimization algorithm with rigorous convergence guarantees. Extensive experiments on benchmark color video inpainting datasets validate the superior performance of the proposed method over existing approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Quaternion Nonlinear Transform-Induced Tensor Nuclear Norm (QNTTNN) method for tensor completion, extending nonlinear TNN models to the quaternion domain to better preserve inter-channel dependencies in color images and videos, with superior performance on color video inpainting tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing linear and nonlinear transform-based TNN methods are limited to real-valued tensors and fail to model quaternion-valued data, which is essential for preserving inter-channel dependencies in color images and videos due to the non-commutativity of quaternion multiplication and complexity of quaternion SVD.
AI.method: The authors propose QNTTNN via a real embedding of quaternions to enable tractable nuclear norm definitions and efficient optimization. They formulate a quaternion tensor completion model and develop a proximal alternating minimization algorithm with rigorous convergence guarantees.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on benchmark color video inpainting datasets demonstrate superior performance of the proposed QNTTNN method over existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: QNTTNN effectively extends nonlinear TNN to the quaternion domain, addressing limitations of prior works and achieving state-of-the-art results for color video inpainting.

title: Gaussian-weighted normal operators on Euclidean space
summary: We consider the normal operator of the X-ray transform, weighted with Gaussian weights, in Euclidean space with dimension at least 3. We show the eigenfunctions of the normal operator are joint eigenfunctions of the harmonic oscillator and the spherical Laplacian, and we relate the spectrum to that of elliptic operators in the 1-cusp pseudodifferential calculus.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the normal operator of the Gaussian-weighted X-ray transform in dimensions ≥3, showing its eigenfunctions are joint eigenfunctions of the harmonic oscillator and spherical Laplacian, with spectrum related to elliptic operators in the 1-cusp pseudodifferential calculus.
AI.motivation: To understand the spectral properties of the weighted X-ray transform normal operator in higher dimensions.
AI.method: Spectral analysis relating the normal operator to the harmonic oscillator and spherical Laplacian, connecting to 1-cusp pseudodifferential calculus.
AI.result: Eigenfunctions of the normal operator are joint eigenfunctions of the harmonic oscillator and spherical Laplacian; the spectrum relates to elliptic operators in the 1-cusp pseudodifferential calculus.
AI.conclusion: The normal operator's spectral structure can be understood through well-known operators and a specialized calculus framework.

title: On weak solutions for the stationary Cahn-Hillard-Navier-Stokes equations with singular potential
summary: The stationary Navier--Stokes--Cahn--Hilliard equations are considered, governing the motion of a compressible, two-phase fluid mixture with a diffuse interface. The free energy density in this paper has a singular logarithmic (Flory-uggins) form, ensuring that the mass fraction remains in the physical range and allowing for vacuum states. We prove the existence of weak solutions in a three-dimensional bounded domain under structural assumptions on the adiabatic exponent. The stationary setting poses two main mathematical challenges: the absence of an energy inequality driven by the evolution process to control the singular potential, and the degeneracy of the density near the vacuum. To address these issues, we introduce a specialized regularization of the logarithmic term that eliminates the quadratic growth induced by anti-diffusion, thereby restoring compactness. Uniform estimates are obtained through a special choice of artificial pressure and an interpolation argument that controls the desired norm of the density. A two-level limiting process then yields a weak solution that satisfies the physical bounds almost everywhere on the support of the density.To our knowledge, this is the first existence result for the steady compressible Navier--Stokes--Cahn--Hilliard system that incorporates both a singular free energy and vacuum regions.

AI.tldr: This paper proves the first existence result for weak solutions of stationary compressible Navier-Stokes-Cahn-Hilliard equations with singular logarithmic free energy and vacuum states in 3D bounded domains.
AI.motivation: To establish the existence of weak solutions for the stationary compressible two-phase flow model with singular (logarithmic) free energy density, which ensures physical mass fraction bounds and allows vacuum states—a problem not previously solved due to mathematical challenges from the stationary setting and density degeneracy.
AI.method: The authors introduce a specialized regularization of the logarithmic term to eliminate quadratic anti-diffusion growth, restore compactness, obtain uniform estimates via artificial pressure and interpolation arguments, and then use a two-level limiting process to recover weak solutions.
AI.result: First existence result for weak solutions of the steady compressible Navier-Stokes-Cahn-Hilliard system with singular free energy and vacuum regions in 3D.
AI.conclusion: The proposed regularization and limiting procedure successfully yield weak solutions that satisfy physical bounds almost everywhere on the density support, overcoming challenges from the stationary setting and vacuum degeneracy.

title: Foundations of Riemannian Geometry for Riemannian Optimization: A Monograph with Detailed Derivations
comment: 143 pages; expository and implementation-oriented monograph with detailed derivations
summary:
Riemannian geometry provides the fundamental framework for optimization on nonlinear spaces such as matrix manifolds, which arise in machine learning, signal processing, and robotics. While the underlying theory is classical, existing literature often presents results at a high level of abstraction, omitting the detailed coordinate-level derivations required for implementation and algorithm development.
  This work provides a self-contained and rigorous treatment of the foundations of Riemannian geometry, with a focus on explicit derivations tailored to Riemannian optimization. We systematically develop the key geometric structures -- including tangent and cotangent spaces, tensor calculus, metric tensors, Levi-Civita connections, curvature, and geodesics -- emphasizing step-by-step derivations in coordinates and matrix form.
  Building on these foundations, we derive the Riemannian gradient, Hessian, exponential map, and retraction in a form suitable for numerical computation. We further specialize these constructions to important matrix manifolds, including the Stiefel, Grassmann, and SPD (Symmetric Positive Definite) manifolds, providing explicit formulas widely used in optimization and geometric machine learning.
  This monograph develops a unified and implementation-oriented treatment of Riemannian geometry for optimization on manifolds. Its main contribution is the systematic organization and detailed derivation of classical geometric constructions in forms directly usable for algorithm design and numerical implementation. By connecting coordinate-level differential geometry with matrix-manifold formulas, the monograph bridges the gap between abstract theory and practical computation, and provides a reference for researchers and practitioners working in Riemannian optimization and related fields.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a self-contained, rigorous treatment of Riemannian geometry foundations with explicit coordinate-level derivations, tailored for Riemannian optimization. It systematically develops key geometric structures (tangent spaces, metric tensors, connections, curvature, geodesics) and derives practical formulas for Riemannian gradient, Hessian, exponential map, and retraction, specializing to Stiefel, Grassmann, and SPD manifolds. The monograph bridges abstract theory and numerical implementation.
AI.motivation: Existing literature on Riemannian geometry is presented at a high level of abstraction, omitting detailed coordinate-level derivations needed for practical implementation and algorithm development in optimization. Practitioners lack a unified, implementation-oriented reference connecting abstract theory to computable formulas.
AI.method: The authors systematically develop Riemannian geometry foundations with step-by-step derivations in coordinates and matrix form, covering tangent/cotangent spaces, tensor calculus, metric tensors, Levi-Civita connections, curvature, and geodesics. They then derive practical optimization tools (Riemannian gradient, Hessian, exponential map, retraction) and specialize to Stiefel, Grassmann, and SPD manifolds.
AI.result: A unified, implementation-oriented monograph presenting classical geometric constructions in forms directly usable for algorithm design and numerical computation, with explicit formulas for key matrix manifolds.
AI.conclusion: The monograph bridges the gap between abstract differential geometry and practical Riemannian optimization by connecting coordinate-level derivations with matrix-manifold formulas, serving as a reference for researchers and practitioners.

title: Sharp regularity for a class of degenerate/singular fully nonlinear elliptic equations with Hamiltonian terms
summary: We investigate the regularity of the viscosity solutions to a class of degenerate/singular fully nonlinear elliptic equations with Hamiltonian terms. To overcome the difficulty caused by the simultaneous presence of the general degenerate/singular gradient terms and Hamiltonian terms, we analyze the coupled interplay between the degeneracy/singularity law and the growth of Hamiltonian terms and establish lower regularity results. Finally, we obtain sharp interior $C^{1,α}$ regularity estimates via a geometric tangential method.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes sharp interior C^{1,α} regularity estimates for viscosity solutions of degenerate/singular fully nonlinear elliptic equations with Hamiltonian terms, using a geometric tangential method and analyzing the interplay between degeneracy/singularity and Hamiltonian growth.
AI.motivation: To understand the regularity of viscosity solutions for a class of degenerate/singular fully nonlinear elliptic equations with Hamiltonian terms, overcoming difficulties from the simultaneous presence of general degenerate/singular gradient terms and Hamiltonian terms.
AI.method: The authors analyze the coupled interplay between the degeneracy/singularity law and the growth of Hamiltonian terms, establish lower regularity results, and then obtain sharp interior C^{1,α} regularity estimates via a geometric tangential method.
AI.result: Sharp interior C^{1,α} regularity estimates are established for viscosity solutions of the considered class of equations.
AI.conclusion: The coupled interplay between degeneracy/singularity law and Hamiltonian growth determines the regularity, and the geometric tangential method yields optimal C^{1,α} estimates.

title: Variational Matrix-Learning Fourier Networks for Parametric Multiphysics Surrogates
summary: Multiphysics simulation is critical for system-technology co-optimization (STCO) in chiplet-based design, but repeated finite-element solutions of PDE-governed problems are computationally expensive in parametric design exploration. This paper proposes a variational matrix-learning Fourier network (VMLFN) for efficient parametric multiphysics surrogate modeling. VMLFN constructs a log-space sine neural representation with randomly sampled spectral frequencies, frequency-dependent decay regulation, and embedded Dirichlet boundary conditions. With fixed hidden-layer parameters, the output-layer weights are determined by reformulating the governing PDEs into variational weak forms and enforcing the stationarity condition of the resulting energy functional. This converts physics-informed training into a linear matrix-solving problem, requiring only first-order derivatives and avoiding both high-order automatic differentiation and penalty-coefficient tuning. A heuristic frequency-scanning algorithm is further introduced to select a problem-adaptive maximum frequency that covers the dominant spectral range of the target problem. The proposed method is validated on heat conduction, solid mechanics, and Helmholtz wave propagation problems. Results from five benchmark cases demonstrate that VMLFN delivers accurate full-field predictions with substantial speedup over conventional physics-informed neural networks and repeated finite-element simulations.

AI.tldr: VMLFN is a physics-informed neural network that replaces expensive finite-element solves in multiphysics parametric design exploration by reformulating PDEs into variational weak forms, solving for output-layer weights via linear matrix equations, using log-space sine representations with frequency scanning for efficient and accurate surrogate modeling.
AI.motivation: Multiphysics simulation in chiplet-based STCO faces high computational cost from repeated finite-element solutions of PDE-governed problems during parametric design exploration.
AI.method: VMLFN uses (1) log-space sine neural representation with random spectral frequencies, (2) frequency-dependent decay regulation and embedded Dirichlet BCs, (3) variational weak form reformulation that converts training into a linear matrix-solving problem (first-order derivatives only, no high-order AD or penalty tuning), and (4) a heuristic frequency-scanning algorithm for adaptive maximum frequency selection.
AI.result: Validated on heat conduction, solid mechanics, and Helmholtz wave propagation across five benchmark cases; achieves accurate full-field predictions with substantial speedup over conventional PINNs and repeated FEM simulations.
AI.conclusion: VMLFN offers an efficient and accurate parametric surrogate modeling framework for multiphysics problems, eliminating costly PDE retraining and tuning overheads, suitable for STCO in chiplet design.

title: Zero-viscosity limit of the chemotaxis-Navier-Stokes equations with the Navier-slip boundary condition
summary: The interplay of chemotaxis and diffusion of nutrients or signaling chemicals in bacterial suspensions can produce a variety of structures with locally high concentrations of cells, including phyllotactic patterns, filaments, and concentrations in fabricated microstructures, which is described by the chemotaxis-Navier-Stokes flow by Tuval et al. in 2005. Dombrowski et al. also observed that Bacterial flow in a sessile drop related to those in the Boycott effect of sedimentation can carry bioconvective plumes, viewed from below through the bottom of a petri dish, and the horizontal "turbulence" white line near the top is the air-water-plastic contact line. It's interesting to verify these turbulent phenomena mathematically. For varying chemotactic and velocity viscosities, we derive the boundary layer equations of the chemotaxis-Navier-Stokes system rigorously in a two-dimensional half-space under the Navier-slip boundary condition and obtain the vanishing viscosity limit of the 2D chemotaxis-fluid coupled system in the anisotropic conormal Sobolev spaces.

AI.tldr: This paper rigorously derives boundary layer equations for the chemotaxis-Navier-Stokes system in a 2D half-space under Navier-slip boundary conditions, and proves the vanishing viscosity limit in anisotropic conormal Sobolev spaces, providing mathematical validation for turbulent phenomena observed in bacterial suspensions.
AI.motivation: To mathematically verify the turbulent bioconvective phenomena observed experimentally in bacterial suspensions (phyllotactic patterns, plumes, Boycott effect-like flows) by rigorously deriving boundary layer equations and establishing vanishing viscosity limits for the chemotaxis-Navier-Stokes system.
AI.method: Derivation of boundary layer equations for the chemotaxis-Navier-Stokes system in a 2D half-space under Navier-slip boundary conditions, with rigorous analysis in anisotropic conormal Sobolev spaces considering varying chemotactic and velocity viscosities.
AI.result: Obtained the vanishing viscosity limit of the 2D chemotaxis-fluid coupled system in anisotropic conormal Sobolev spaces, providing a rigorous mathematical foundation for understanding bacterial turbulent patterns observed in experiments.
AI.conclusion: The mathematical framework successfully captures the boundary layer dynamics of bacterial suspensions, confirming that the observed bioconvective turbulence can be rigorously described through the chemotaxis-Navier-Stokes system with appropriate boundary conditions.

title: ZNO: Stable Rational Neural Operators in the Z-Domain for Discrete-Time Dynamic
summary: We introduce the Z-Domain Neural Operator (ZNO), a causal neural operator whose layers are stable low-rank multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) rational filters parameterized directly in the $z$-plane. ZNO addresses a limitation of existing operator learning methods, many of which are primarily tailored for continuous-time problems, while a large class of system-identification problems is intrinsically discrete-time. The $z$-domain form expresses stability as a unit-disk pole constraint and makes learned discrete-time poles directly readable. The model combines low-rank channel mixing, smooth stable pole reparameterization, causal recurrence, and an optional short finite impulse response (FIR) branch in a single $z$-domain rational recurrent layer. Across controlled discrete system-identification experiments, ZNO's advantage is most evident when the target dynamics are stable rational systems with lightly damped poles near the unit circle. Under matched parameter budgets, ZNO is not uniformly dominant; however, with validation-selected configurations, the same architecture can achieve the lowest mean error across the controlled tasks. A five-bin difficulty sweep over near-unit-circle / long-memory dynamics shows that ZNO has the lowest mean error across memory regimes, from short (approximately 10 steps) to long (approximately 100-200 steps). On five public nonlinear system-identification benchmarks, ZNO is competitive with neural operator and state-space baselines, achieving the lowest mean error on benchmarks whose dynamics align with stable rational discrete-time filters, while classical or state-space baselines remain preferable on some systems. These results position ZNO as a strong model for stable rational discrete-time dynamics, especially in near-unit-circle and long-memory regimes, but not as a universal replacement for specialized system-identification methods.

AI.tldr: ZNO is a causal neural operator with stable low-rank MIMO rational filters in the z-plane, designed for discrete-time system identification, excelling on stable rational systems with lightly damped/near-unit-circle poles and long-memory dynamics, though not a universal replacement for specialized methods.
AI.motivation: Existing operator learning methods are mainly tailored for continuous-time problems, while many system-identification problems are intrinsically discrete-time. This paper aims to address that gap.
AI.method: ZNO uses low-rank stable rational MIMO filters parameterized in the z-plane, with unit-disk pole constraints, causal recurrence, and an optional short FIR branch within a single rational recurrent layer.
AI.result: ZNO excels on stable rational systems with lightly damped/near-unit-circle poles and long-memory dynamics (100-200 steps). It achieves competitive or lowest mean error on certain benchmarks but is not uniformly dominant across all tasks.
AI.conclusion: ZNO is a strong model for stable rational discrete-time dynamics (especially near-unit-circle and long-memory regimes) but not a universal replacement for specialized system-identification methods.

title: Gradient estimates for degenerate elliptic measure data problems with double phase
summary: We study nonlinear elliptic equations modeled on \[ -\mathrm{div}\,(|Du|^{p-2}Du+a(x)|Du|^{q-2}Du) = μ, \] where $2\le p<q<\infty$, $a(\cdot) \ge 0$, and $μ$ is a signed Borel measure with finite total mass. We prove local Calderón--Zygmund type gradient estimates for SOLA (Solutions Obtained as Limits of Approximations) by finding new and natural assumptions on $p$, $q$ and $a(\cdot)$.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes Calderón-Zygmund gradient estimates for SOLA to a class of nonlinear elliptic equations with measure data, under new natural conditions on parameters p, q and coefficient a(x).
AI.motivation: To obtain gradient regularity estimates (Calderón-Zygmund type) for solutions of certain nonlinear elliptic equations with measure data, under the weakest/natural possible assumptions on the exponents p, q and the coefficient a(x).
AI.method: The paper studies SOLA (Solutions Obtained as Limits of Approximations) and derives local Calderón-Zygmund type gradient estimates by imposing new and natural assumptions on p, q (with 2 ≤ p < q < ∞) and a(x) ≥ 0.
AI.result: Local Calderón-Zygmund type gradient estimates are proved for SOLA under new, natural assumptions on p, q and a(x).
AI.conclusion: The authors successfully establish gradient regularity estimates for this class of equations, relaxing or improving earlier assumptions on the parameters and coefficient.

title: Fast and accurate conditioning for large-scale and online Gaussian process prediction problems
summary: Gaussian Process (GP) models provide a flexible framework for prediction and uncertainty quantification. For most covariance functions, however, exact GP prediction with $n$ points scales as $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$, making it prohibitively expensive for large datasets or large numbers of prediction points. While nearest neighbor-based prediction can work well in certain settings, non-pathological circumstances (for example measurement noise) can severely restrict its efficiency. This work presents a complementary approach where one conditions on carefully designed linear combinations of data, which is particularly effective in the setting of predicting many values in large connected regions of the data domain. For kernel functions that are smooth away from the origin, conditioning on a small number $r$ of such data contrasts can be machine-precision accurate for the full exact conditional distributions. These contrasts cost $\mathcal{O}(T r^2)$ work to compute where $T$ is the cost of solving a linear system with the data covariance matrix, and so in many cases can be computed in linear or near-linear cost by exploiting rank structure in well-behaved covariance matrices. At the cost of $\mathcal{O}(nr^2)$ additional precomputation work, this approach can also provide predictions at arbitrary points of a designated region in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ online work, making it particularly attractive for problems where prediction points are not known in advance.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a method to accelerate Gaussian Process predictions by conditioning on carefully designed linear combinations of data (contrasts), achieving machine-precision accuracy with O(Tr²) computation and O(1) online prediction cost.
AI.motivation: Exact GP prediction scales O(n³), and nearest neighbor methods fail under non-pathological conditions like measurement noise. The authors aim to develop a complementary, efficient approach for large-scale GP prediction.
AI.method: The method conditions on a small number r of data contrasts (linear combinations) rather than raw nearest neighbors. It exploits rank structure in covariance matrices for O(Tr²) computation, plus O(nr²) precomputation for O(1) online prediction at arbitrary points.
AI.result: The approach achieves machine-precision accuracy for full exact conditional distributions with only r contrasts, and enables O(1) online prediction after precomputation, making it highly efficient for large connected prediction regions.
AI.conclusion: This contrast-based conditioning approach complements existing methods, offering linear or near-linear cost for many covariance matrices, and is especially suitable when many prediction points in large regions are needed or are unknown in advance.

title: On the observability of the Schrödinger equation in the torus from open sets
summary: We study the observability of the Schrödinger equation on the $d$-dimensional torus $\mathbb T^d$, $d \geq 1$, from an open subset $ω\subset \mathbb T^d$. Our first main result establishes a quantitative observability estimate for the free Schrödinger equation in the regime of small times $T$ and for small observation sets of the form $ω= \prod_{j=1}^{d}(a_j,b_j)$. Our second main result shows that observability holds for the Schrödinger equation with a merely bounded potential $V \in L^{\infty}(\mathbb T^d)$, in any dimension $d \geq 1$, for every time $T>0$ and every nonempty open subset $ω$. This resolves a well-known conjecture in the field. A central ingredient in the proof is a cluster decomposition method combined with an induction scheme introduced by Bourgain and further developed by Burq and Zhu.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes quantitative observability estimates for the free Schrödinger equation on the torus and proves that observability holds for Schrödinger equations with bounded potentials for any time and any open subset, resolving a longstanding conjecture.
AI.motivation: To resolve a well-known conjecture about observability of the Schrödinger equation with bounded potentials on the torus, and to establish quantitative estimates for the free case with small observation sets and small times.
AI.method: The proof uses a cluster decomposition method combined with an induction scheme originally introduced by Bourgain and further developed by Burq and Zhu.
AI.result: (1) A quantitative observability estimate for the free Schrödinger equation for small times and small observation sets of product form. (2) Observability holds for the Schrödinger equation with bounded potential V ∈ L∞(𝕋ᵈ) in any dimension d≥1, for every time T>0 and every nonempty open subset ω.
AI.conclusion: The conjecture that Schrödinger equations with bounded potentials are observable on the torus for any time and any open set is affirmatively resolved, advancing the understanding of controllability and inverse problems for such systems.

title: A second-order method on the Stiefel manifold via Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz
summary: Retraction-free approaches offer attractive low-cost alternatives to Riemannian methods on the Stiefel manifold, but they are often first-order, which may limit the efficiency under high-accuracy requirements. To this end, we propose a second-order method landing on the Stiefel manifold without invoking retractions, which is proved to enjoy local quadratic (or superlinear for its inexact variant) convergence. The update consists of the sum of (i) a component tangent to the level set of the constraint-defining function that aims to reduce the objective and (ii) a component normal to the same level set that reduces the infeasibility. Specifically, we construct the normal component via Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz, a fixed-point iteration for orthogonalization. Moreover, we establish a geometric connection between the Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz iteration and Stiefel manifolds, in which Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz moves along the normal space. For the tangent component, we formulate a modified Newton equation that incorporates Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz. Numerical experiments on the orthogonal Procrustes problem, principal component analysis, and real-data independent component analysis illustrate that the proposed method performs better than the existing methods.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a second-order method for optimization on the Stiefel manifold that avoids retractions, using Newton-Schulz iteration for orthogonalization and a modified Newton equation for the tangent component, achieving local quadratic convergence and outperforming existing methods on several tasks.
AI.motivation: Retraction-free approaches offer low-cost alternatives to Riemannian methods on the Stiefel manifold but are often first-order, limiting efficiency under high-accuracy requirements.
AI.method: The update consists of (i) a tangent component that reduces the objective via a modified Newton equation incorporating Newton-Schulz, and (ii) a normal component that reduces infeasibility via Newton-Schulz fixed-point iteration for orthogonalization, with a geometric connection established between Newton-Schulz and the Stiefel manifold's normal space.
AI.result: The method achieves local quadratic convergence (or superlinear for its inexact variant). Numerical experiments on orthogonal Procrustes, PCA, and real-data ICA show better performance than existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed second-order retraction-free method for Stiefel manifold optimization is both theoretically convergent at quadratic rate and practically effective across multiple optimization tasks.

title: On the onset of correlations in Wave Turbulence close to singularities
summary: In this paper we describe in a formal way how the derivation of the turbulent wave equation for the Schrödinger equation breaks down for times close to the self similar blow up of the wave turbulence kinetic equation. To this end, we study how the derivation of the cumulants hierarchy can not be approximated using solutions of the wave turbulence kinetic equation near the blow up time. It tuns out that near the blow up time the kinetic equation has to be replaced by a hierarchy of equations which is equivalent to a random field, defined for times $t\in (-\infty, \infty)$ and satisfying a nonlinear non autonomous Schrödinger equation.

AI.tldr: This paper formally shows that the derivation of the turbulent wave equation for the Schrödinger equation fails near the self-similar blow-up time of the wave turbulence kinetic equation, requiring replacement with a random field satisfying a nonlinear non-autonomous Schrödinger equation.
AI.motivation: To understand and formalize the breakdown of the turbulent wave equation derivation when approaching the self-similar blow-up time in wave turbulence theory.
AI.method: Formal analysis of the cumulant hierarchy derivation, showing that the wave turbulence kinetic equation approximation fails near the blow-up time, and deriving a replacement hierarchy equivalent to a random field described by a nonlinear non-autonomous Schrödinger equation.
AI.result: The wave turbulence kinetic equation must be replaced by a hierarchy of equations equivalent to a random field defined for all times t∈(−∞,∞), satisfying a nonlinear non-autonomous Schrödinger equation near the blow-up time.
AI.conclusion: The standard wave turbulence kinetic approximation breaks down near blow-up time, necessitating a more complex random field description governed by a non-autonomous nonlinear Schrödinger equation.

title: On Spectral multiplier theorem for sub-Laplacians with drift on Métivier groups
summary: In this paper, we prove a spectral multiplier theorem for sub-Laplacians with drift on Métivier groups. We improve the result of [Martini, Ottazzi and Vallarino, Rev. Mat. Iberoam, 2019] in case of Métivier groups, by reducing the required smoothness condition on the multiplier function from homogeneous dimension to the topological dimension of the underlying group.

AI.tldr: The paper proves a spectral multiplier theorem for sub-Laplacians with drift on Métivier groups, improving previous results by reducing the required smoothness condition from homogeneous dimension to topological dimension.
AI.motivation: To improve the existing spectral multiplier theorem for sub-Laplacians with drift on Métivier groups by reducing the smoothness requirement on the multiplier function.
AI.method: The authors refine the spectral multiplier theorem previously established by Martini, Ottazzi, and Vallarino (2019), specifically for Métivier groups, by lowering the smoothness condition from the homogeneous dimension to the topological dimension.
AI.result: The required smoothness condition on the multiplier function is reduced from the homogeneous dimension to the topological dimension of the underlying group.
AI.conclusion: The new spectral multiplier theorem for sub-Laplacians with drift on Métivier groups requires less smoothness on the multiplier function than previously known, matching the topological dimension rather than the higher homogeneous dimension.

title: Quantitative homogenization of elliptic equations with infinitely many scales
summary: In this paper, we develop a general homogenization theory for elliptic equations with coefficients that oscillate periodically at infinitely many scales $\varepsilon = (\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2, \cdots) \in (0,1)^\infty$, with $\varepsilon_1>\varepsilon_2>\cdots$ and $\varepsilon_n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$. Such problems arise naturally in the study of fractal materials and diffusion in fluids. Under suitable scale-separation assumptions, we prove a qualitative homogenization theorem and obtain optimal $L^2$ convergence rates. We also establish interior and boundary Lipschitz estimates that are uniform in $\varepsilon$.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a homogenization theory for elliptic equations with periodic oscillations at infinitely many scales, proving convergence results and optimal rates for fractal materials and fluid diffusion.
AI.motivation: To understand effective (macroscopic) behavior of materials with complex microscale structures exhibiting periodic oscillations at infinitely many scales, as seen in fractal materials and diffusion in fluids.
AI.method: General homogenization theory using scale-separation assumptions; qualitative homogenization theorem; optimal L² convergence rate estimates; uniform interior and boundary Lipschitz estimates independent of the multiscale parameter ε.
AI.result: Proved a qualitative homogenization theorem; obtained optimal L² convergence rates; established interior and boundary Lipschitz estimates uniform in ε.
AI.conclusion: The developed theory successfully handles infinitely many microscales, bridging multiple-scale homogenization and providing rigorous convergence guarantees with optimal rates.

title: The obstacle problem for singular quasi-linear elliptic equations
summary: Existence of solutions to an obstacle $p$-Laplacian problem exhibiting a singular, discontinuous reaction is proved. The reaction term may be discontinuous in a Lebesgue-negligible set. Moreover, solutions are shown to be locally $C^{1,α}$ far away from the contact set. Under a differentiability hypothesis on the obstacle, solutions belong to $C^{1,α}(\overlineΩ)$.

AI.tldr: This paper proves existence of solutions to an obstacle p-Laplacian problem with singular, discontinuous reaction terms, and establishes local C^{1,α} regularity away from the contact set, with global C^{1,α} regularity under additional differentiability conditions on the obstacle.
AI.motivation: To establish existence and regularity results for obstacle p-Laplacian problems that involve singular and discontinuous reaction terms, which are challenging due to the lack of smoothness in the nonlinearity.
AI.method: The analysis likely employs variational or PDE techniques (e.g., sub-supersolution methods, approximation schemes, or regularity theory) to handle the singular and discontinuous reaction, combined with obstacle problem constraints.
AI.result: Existence of solutions is proved for the obstacle p-Laplacian problem with a reaction that may be discontinuous on a Lebesgue-negligible set. Solutions are locally C^{1,α} away from the contact set, and globally C^{1,α} when the obstacle is differentiable.
AI.conclusion: The work extends the theory of obstacle problems to include highly irregular reactions while still achieving optimal regularity results.
