title: A stiff limit of non-homogeneous conservation laws for crowd motion modeling
summary: We propose a new approach for crowd motion models where the density constraint can only slow down the motion of each agent, with no effect on those agents who are not in a saturated area or who have no saturated density ''in front'' of them. This is done by means of a limit of conservation laws inspired by the equations used for traffic as in Follow the leader-type models. We study the asymptotics of the solutions of these conservation laws in a certain asymptotic regime, and obtain a PDE at the limit of a whole new type. One of the main goals of the paper is to prove uniform BV estimates on the density, and thus strong compactness to prove the existence of solutions to this limit equation. We also discuss the qualitative behavior of solutions, provide numerical illustrations both in dimension 1 and 2, and establish the new entropy inequalities associated with this limit equation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a new PDE-based crowd motion model where density constraints only slow down agents in saturated areas, derived from conservation laws inspired by traffic flow models, with proven BV estimates, entropy inequalities, and numerical demonstrations in 1D and 2D.
AI.motivation: To develop a crowd motion model that allows density constraints to selectively slow agents only when they are in or moving toward saturated areas, overcoming limitations of existing models that impose uniform constraints.
AI.method: The approach uses a limit of conservation laws inspired by Follow the leader-type traffic models. The authors study asymptotic regimes, prove uniform BV estimates on density for strong compactness, and derive a novel PDE limit with associated entropy inequalities.
AI.result: A new type of PDE at the limit is obtained, with proven existence of solutions via compactness from uniform BV estimates, and entropy inequalities established for the limit equation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed model successfully captures anisotropic density constraints (only affecting agents with saturated density ahead), with theoretical guarantees and numerical validation in both 1D and 2D.

title: An Inverse Source Problem For a Time-Fractional Mixed Wave-Diffusion-Wave Equation in a Cylindrical Domain
summary: This paper addresses the inverse source problem for a mixed-type fractional wave-diffusion-wave equation posed in a cylindrical domain. The governing equation involves a time-dependent variable-order fractional derivative, which enables the model to effectively capture temporal transitions between wave-like and diffusive behaviors. The solution is constructed in the form of a Fourier-Bessel series. By employing the method of separation of variables together with fundamental properties of Bessel functions, we analyze the uniform convergence of the resulting infinite series. This analysis ultimately leads to a rigorous proof of the existence of a solution.

AI.tldr: This paper solves the inverse source problem for a mixed-type fractional wave-diffusion-wave equation with variable-order derivatives in a cylindrical domain, using Fourier-Bessel series and separation of variables to prove solution existence via uniform convergence analysis.
AI.motivation: To address the inverse source problem for a mixed-type fractional wave-diffusion-wave equation with time-dependent variable-order fractional derivatives, enabling the model to capture transitions between wave and diffusive behaviors.
AI.method: The solution is constructed using Fourier-Bessel series. The method of separation of variables and fundamental properties of Bessel functions are employed to analyze uniform convergence of the infinite series.
AI.result: A rigorous proof of the existence of a solution is established through uniform convergence analysis of the Fourier-Bessel series.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully proves solution existence for the inverse source problem in a mixed-type fractional wave-diffusion-wave equation in cylindrical domains.

title: A Shape Design Approximation for Degenerate Partial Differential Equations and Its Application
summary: In this paper, we focus on two types of degenerate partial differential equations: a degenerate elliptic equation and a degenerate parabolic equation. Significantly, both categories are characterized by the same principal operator. To obtain solutions for these equations, we introduce a novel approximation approach, termed the shape design approximation. As a practical application of this method, we derive a Carleman estimate for the backward degenerate parabolic equation. This estimate plays a pivotal role in establishing the null controllability of the degenerate parabolic equation. A notable advantage of employing the shape design approximation in deriving the Carleman estimate is that it enables us to bypass the requirement for second order derivatives in the degenerate equation. Usually, this has been a significant obstacle in the derivation of Carleman estimates for degenerate parabolic equations.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel "shape design approximation" to solve degenerate elliptic and parabolic PDEs sharing the same principal operator, deriving a Carleman estimate for the backward degenerate parabolic equation that enables null controllability while bypassing the need for second-order derivatives—a traditional obstacle in this field.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of deriving Carleman estimates for degenerate parabolic equations, which has been hindered by the requirement for second-order derivatives in the degenerate equation.
AI.method: A novel approximation approach called "shape design approximation" is introduced to handle degenerate elliptic and parabolic equations characterized by the same principal operator.
AI.result: A Carleman estimate for the backward degenerate parabolic equation is derived, enabling the establishment of null controllability for the degenerate parabolic equation.
AI.conclusion: The shape design approximation successfully bypasses the requirement for second-order derivatives, which has typically been a significant obstacle in deriving Carleman estimates for degenerate parabolic equations.

title: Quantitative Weak Unique Continuation on Annular Domains for Backward Degenerate Parabolic Equations with Degenerate Interior Points
summary: In this paper, we establish a quantitative weak unique continuation theorem on an annular domain for a backward degenerate parabolic equation with a degenerate interior point. Our methodology hinges on approximating the solution of the degenerate parabolic equation through solutions of non-degenerate parabolic counterparts. Subsequently, we establish Carleman estimates for the non-degenerate parabolic equation across two separate domains. By virtue of these estimates, we deduce a quantitative weak unique continuation property for the degenerate parabolic equation, thereby substantiating the weak unique continuation result for the original degenerate parabolic equation.

AI.tldr: This paper proves a quantitative weak unique continuation theorem for a backward degenerate parabolic equation with an interior degenerate point, using Carleman estimates derived from non-degenerate parabolic approximations.
AI.motivation: To establish a weak unique continuation property for degenerate parabolic equations that have a degenerate interior point, which is nontrivial due to the degeneracy.
AI.method: Approximate the degenerate parabolic solution with non-degenerate counterparts, then derive Carleman estimates for the non-degenerate equation over two separate domains.
AI.result: A quantitative weak unique continuation property is obtained for the degenerate parabolic equation, supporting the weak unique continuation result.
AI.conclusion: The weak unique continuation property holds for backward degenerate parabolic equations with interior degeneracy, validated via Carleman estimates from non-degenerate approximations.

title: Sharp regularity for degenerate fully nonlinear equations with oblique boundary conditions and Hamiltonian terms
summary: We prove optimal boundary $C^{1,α}$ regularity for viscosity solutions of degenerate fully nonlinear uniformly elliptic equations with oblique boundary conditions and Hamiltonian terms of the form \[ \begin{cases} |Du|^γF(D^2 u) + \varrho(x)|Du|^σ = f(x) & \text{in } Ω,\\ β(x)\cdot Du+ζ(x)u = g(x) & \text{on } \partial Ω, \end{cases} \] where $γ>0$ and $0<σ\le 1+γ$. We develop a compactness framework for affine translations, linking the size of the translation to the Hamiltonian structure. This is combined with a boundary improvement-of-flatness argument adapted to oblique boundary data, yielding the optimal boundary regularity.

AI.tldr: The paper proves optimal boundary C^{1,α} regularity for viscosity solutions of degenerate fully nonlinear elliptic equations with oblique boundary conditions and Hamiltonian terms, using a compactness framework for affine translations and a boundary improvement-of-flatness argument.
AI.motivation: To establish optimal boundary regularity for a class of degenerate fully nonlinear elliptic PDEs with oblique boundary conditions, which is important for understanding the behavior of solutions near boundaries in complex nonlinear systems.
AI.method: A compactness framework for affine translations is developed, linking translation size to the Hamiltonian structure, combined with a boundary improvement-of-flatness argument adapted to oblique boundary data.
AI.result: Optimal boundary C^{1,α} regularity is proven for viscosity solutions of the given degenerate equations under oblique boundary conditions.
AI.conclusion: The developed compactness approach effectively handles the coupling between degenerate ellipticity, Hamiltonian structure, and oblique boundary conditions, achieving sharp regularity estimates.

title: Blow-up analysis and a priori bounds for NLS equations on metric graphs
summary:
We consider, on a connected metric graph $\mathcal{G}$, a family of nonlinear Schrödinger equations $$
  -u'' + W_n(x) u + λ_n u = ρ_n(x)|u|^{p-2}u,
  \quad n \in \mathbb{N}.
  \qquad (*) $$ We assume that $p > 2$, $(W_n)$, $(ρ_n) \subseteq L^{\infty}(\mathcal{G})$ with $ρ_n \geq 0$, $|W_n|_{L^\infty(\mathcal{G})}$ and $|ρ_n|_{L^\infty(\mathcal{G})}$ are bounded and $λ_n \to +\infty$. Given $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we call "solution" a function $u_n \in H^1(\mathcal{G})$ which satisfies (*) for that $n\in \mathbb{N}$ together with the Kirchhoff conditions at the vertices. Focusing on the limiting behavior of sequences $(u_n) \subseteq H^1(\mathcal{G})$ of solutions as $λ_n \to + \infty$ and assuming that the Morse index $m(u_n)$ of $u_n$ is uniformly bounded, we establish, the existence of a finite subset of blow-up points away from which, up to a subsequence, $|u_n|$ has a global exponential decay. These points are generally a strict subset of the blow-up points, and their number is estimated by the bound on the Morse index of $(u_n)$. It is the first time that this global exponential decay property is established on graphs even if one consider only signed solutions. In the last part of the paper we derive various results of a priori bounds on the solutions in $L^\infty$ and $L^2$. Our blow-up analysis, combined with ODE arguments allows, for frequently considered classes of graphs, to obtain a fairly complete picture of the relationships between the number of nodal regions, Morse index, $L^\infty$ and $L^2$ norms of solutions.

AI.tldr: This paper studies nonlinear Schrödinger equations on metric graphs, proving that sequences of solutions with uniformly bounded Morse index exhibit global exponential decay away from a finite set of blow-up points as λₙ→∞, and establishes a priori bounds linking Morse index, nodal regions, and L²/L∞ norms.
AI.motivation: To understand the limiting behavior of solutions to nonlinear Schrödinger equations on metric graphs as a parameter λₙ tends to infinity, focusing on blow-up phenomena and exponential decay properties that were previously unestablished for graphs.
AI.method: The authors analyze sequences of solutions (uₙ) in H¹(𝒢) with Kirchhoff boundary conditions, assuming uniformly bounded Morse index. They use blow-up analysis combined with ODE arguments to identify a finite subset of blow-up points, prove global exponential decay of |uₙ| away from these points, and derive a priori L∞ and L² bounds.
AI.result: (1) Existence of a finite set of blow-up points with global exponential decay of |uₙ| away from them; (2) The number of these points is bounded by the Morse index bound; (3) A priori L∞ and L² bounds for solutions; (4) Relationships between nodal regions, Morse index, and solution norms on common graph classes.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes for the first time on metric graphs the global exponential decay property for solutions with uniformly bounded Morse index, and provides a comprehensive characterization of the interplay between key solution features (Morse index, nodal count, norm bounds) on frequently studied graph classes.

title: Stackelberg-Nash controllability for a multi-objective Stefan problem
summary: We investigate a hierarchical control problem for a one-dimensional Stefan system with localized distributed controls. The setting combines a Stackelberg strategy with a Nash equilibrium among multiple followers, yielding a multi-objective free-boundary problem. The interaction between the hierarchical control and the moving interface results in a nonlinear optimality system, and we show that the original problem reduces to the null controllability of this optimality system. Under suitable geometric conditions on the control regions, we establish a local null controllability result. The proof relies on an observability inequality for a linearized system, obtained through Carleman estimates adapted to the presence of a moving boundary. These results constitute, to the best of our knowledge, the first treatment of a Stefan system within a Stackelberg-Nash framework.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates a hierarchical control problem for a one-dimensional Stefan system using a Stackelberg-Nash strategy, reducing it to null controllability of an optimality system and proving local null controllability via Carleman estimates with moving boundaries.
AI.motivation: To address a multi-objective free-boundary problem combining Stackelberg (leader-follower) strategy with Nash equilibrium among multiple followers in a Stefan system, which has not been previously studied.
AI.method: The problem is reduced to null controllability of a nonlinear optimality system; the proof uses Carleman estimates adapted to moving boundaries to establish an observability inequality for a linearized system.
AI.result: Under suitable geometric conditions on control regions, local null controllability is established for the Stefan system within the Stackelberg-Nash framework.
AI.conclusion: This is the first treatment of a Stefan system in a Stackelberg-Nash framework, demonstrating that hierarchical multi-objective control is feasible for moving-boundary problems.

title: Poincaré type J-equation
summary: We introduce a two-parameter continuity path for the J-equation and use it to characterize the solvability of the J-equation for Kähler metrics with Poincaré type singularities along a divisor $D$, allowing simple normal crossings and self-intersections. On Kähler surfaces, we show that the classical subsolution condition in the smooth setting implies solvability in the Poincaré type setting for any smooth divisor $D$. As a consequence, if $X$ contains no curves of negative self-intersections and $K_X[D]$ is ample, then the K-energy is bounded from below on any Poincaré type Kähler class. In the smooth divisor case, we further analyze the asymptotic behavior of solutions near $D$, and show that existence of a Poincaré type solution implies existence of a solution to the J-equation on $D$.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a continuity path for the J-equation to characterize solvability with Poincaré-type singularities along divisors, showing that classical subsolutions imply Poincaré-type solvability on surfaces and linking to K-energy boundedness and divisor asymptotics.
AI.motivation: To extend the solvability theory of the J-equation to Kähler metrics with Poincaré-type singularities along divisors, and to understand the relationship between smooth and singular settings.
AI.method: A two-parameter continuity path for the J-equation is introduced and analyzed in the context of Poincaré-type singularities along a divisor D with simple normal crossings and self-intersections.
AI.result: On Kähler surfaces, the classical subsolution condition implies solvability in the Poincaré-type setting; if X has no negative self-intersection curves and K_X[D] is ample, K-energy is bounded below; existence of Poincaré-type solution implies a solution on D.
AI.conclusion: The results connect smooth and singular J-equation solvability, providing implications for K-energy boundedness and divisor geometry.

title: Yvonne Choquet-Bruhat 1923-2025
summary: This is a memorial article for Yvonne Choquet-Bruhat, who was one of the great pioneers of mathematical general relativity and of partial differential equations. Starting with her 1952 result on local existence of solutions of the vacuum Einstein field equations, she obtained many results on the Einstein evolution equations, the Einstein constraint equations, and the equations of supergravity. Her methods have also been important for numerical relativity. She also wrote several textbooks and a memoir. An abridged version of this article has been submitted to AMS Notices.

AI.tldr: This is a memorial article honoring Yvonne Choquet-Bruhat, a pioneer in mathematical general relativity and PDEs, highlighting her groundbreaking 1952 local existence result for vacuum Einstein equations and her many contributions to relativity, supergravity, and numerical methods.
AI.motivation: To commemorate and document the life and scientific contributions of Yvonne Choquet-Bruhat, a pioneering figure in mathematical general relativity and partial differential equations.
AI.method: The paper is a memorial/obituary-style article summarizing Choquet-Bruhat's key research contributions, including her work on Einstein evolution equations, Einstein constraint equations, supergravity equations, and her influence on numerical relativity, as well as her textbooks and memoir.
AI.result: Not applicable in the traditional sense; this is a memorial article summarizing her life's work rather than presenting new research results. Key milestone: her 1952 local existence proof for vacuum Einstein equations.
AI.conclusion: Yvonne Choquet-Bruhat was a great pioneer whose work fundamentally shaped mathematical general relativity and PDE theory, with lasting impact on both theoretical and numerical relativity.

title: Robert V. Kohn (1953-2026)
summary: The article is dedicated to the memory and enduring legacy of Professor Robert V. Kohn, Courant Institute, NYU. In this memorial article, we record thoughts and reminiscences of his exemplary life.

AI.tldr: This is a memorial article honoring Professor Robert V. Kohn of the Courant Institute, NYU, reflecting on his life and legacy.
AI.motivation: To honor and remember the life, contributions, and legacy of Professor Robert V. Kohn.
AI.method: Recording thoughts and reminiscences about his exemplary life.
AI.result: A collection of personal reflections and memories celebrating Professor Kohn.
AI.conclusion: Professor Robert V. Kohn is remembered for his exemplary life and lasting impact.

title: Weighted decoupling with lower-dimensional frequency localization
summary: We prove weighted $L^2$ and refined $L^p$ decoupling estimates for functions whose Fourier transforms are supported in a small neighborhood of the unit sphere or the truncated paraboloid with an additional lower-dimensional frequency localization property. As a special case, we recover the fractal $L^2$ restriction estimate of Du and Zhang, with a sharper dependence on the density of the weight. We also derive weighted refined decoupling estimates related to the Falconer distance set problem, improving earlier results under the stronger assumption that the underlying weight is $α$-dimensional at every scale.

AI.tldr: This paper proves weighted L^2 and refined L^p decoupling estimates for functions with Fourier support near the unit sphere or truncated paraboloid, with additional low-dimensional frequency localization. It improves the fractal L^2 restriction estimate of Du and Zhang and advances results related to the Falconer distance set problem.
AI.motivation: To sharpen decoupling estimates under lower-dimensional frequency localization conditions and improve existing results in fractal restriction theory and the Falconer distance set problem.
AI.method: Weighted L^2 and refined L^p decoupling estimates are derived for Fourier transforms supported near the unit sphere or truncated paraboloid, with an extra lower-dimensional frequency localization property.
AI.result: Recovers the fractal L^2 restriction estimate of Du and Zhang with a sharper dependence on weight density. Also improves weighted refined decoupling estimates for the Falconer distance set problem under α-dimensional weight assumptions at every scale.
AI.conclusion: The refined decoupling estimates yield stronger results for fractal restriction and distance set problems than previously known.
